layer: Source extraction & decryption (MegaCloud / RapidCloud) — the crypto chokepoint
overview: MegaCloud (formerly RapidCloud / rabbitstream, the embed host behind zoro.to -> aniwatch.to -> hianime) hides the actual .m3u8 behind one AJAX call, getSources?id=<embedId>, that returns the video "sources" as an AES-encrypted, base64 string instead of plaintext when {encrypted:true}. The entire difficulty of building a free anime site sits here: you must recover the per-deploy decryption key from obfuscated client JS or from a community key feed, then decrypt locally. The scheme rotated through four distinct eras, each breaking every existing scraper: (1) early — key embedded/derivable from the embed HTML or the e1-player.min.js bundle (RapidCloud used CryptoJS OpenSSL 'Salted__' AES-256-CBC with an MD5 EvpKDF); (2) RapidCloud/early-MegaCloud — the key bytes were not a string but a list of [index,length] slices carved out of the ciphertext itself, with the slice-position table fetched from a community GitHub repo (raw.githubusercontent.com/cinemaxhq/keys/e1/key); (3) MegaCloud v2 (megacloud.blog/embed-2/v2/e-1/getSources) — a single rotating 64-hex AES passphrase published to community key repos (itzzzme/megacloud-keys/key.txt, decrypt with plain CryptoJS.AES.decrypt(sources,key)); (4) MegaCloud v3 (megacloud.blog/embed-2/v3/e-1/getSources?id=..&_k=<clientKey>) — a per-request clientKey/nonce scraped out of the embed HTML PLUS a server 'mega' master key from a key repo (yogesh-hacker/MegacloudKeys/keys.json), combined and fed into a bespoke, non-CryptoJS 3-layer cipher (custom LCG-seeded substitution + columnar transposition), with key-gen logic increasingly hidden in WASM/obfuscated player JS. Because the key surface keeps moving, the two surviving maintained projects diverged: ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch keeps re-implementing each era in megacloud.ts (and ships a megaplay.buzz fallback), while consumet.ts gave up on doing crypto in-process and now proxies the whole embed URL to a remote black-box decryptor (crawlr.cc) that returns already-decrypted sources. As of the 2026 hianime shutdown, several key repos went dark (yogesh-hacker keys.json now 404s) and the canonical GitHub repos were DMCA-blocked (HTTP 451), so working knowledge survives mainly in npm-published build artifacts (aniwatch 2.27.9, @consumet/extensions 1.8.8) and forks/mirrors.

components.name: getSources AJAX endpoint
components.role: The single chokepoint request. Returns JSON {sources, tracks, intro, outro, encrypted}. When encrypted=true, sources is an AES/custom-cipher base64 blob; when false, sources is plaintext {file,type}. Path encodes the era/version.
examples: Legacy/RapidCloud: https://megacloud.tv/embed-2/ajax/e-1/getSources?id=<id> (sent with X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest and Referer = embed URL)
v2: https://megacloud.blog/embed-2/v2/e-1/getSources?id=<sourceId>
v3 (latest): https://megacloud.blog/embed-2/v3/e-1/getSources?id=<sourceId>&_k=<clientKey>
Response shape: { sources: "<base64-cipher>", tracks:[{file,kind,label}], intro:{start,end}, outro:{start,end}, encrypted:true }

components.name: embedId / sourceId (the id param)
components.role: Opaque per-episode-per-server token (a.k.a. xrax) parsed from the embed iframe URL; identifies which stream getSources resolves.
examples: Parsed via regex /\/([^\/\?]+)\?/ on the iframe href (e.g. .../e-1/1hnXq7VzX0Ex?k=1 -> 1hnXq7VzX0Ex)
Used as ?id= on getSources

components.name: clientKey / nonce (_k param, v3 era)
components.role: Per-request secret scraped out of the embed-2/v3/e-1/<xrax> HTML and echoed back as &_k=. MegaCloud rotated WHERE it hides this across builds, so the scraper tries a battery of regexes (meta tag, HTML comment, window._lk_db {x,y,z}, data-dpi attr, <script nonce=...>, window._xy_ws). Combined with the master key during keygen.
examples: Regex battery in getMegaCloudClientKey: /<meta name="_gg_fb" content="([a-zA-Z0-9]+)">/, /<!--\s+_is_th:([0-9a-zA-Z]+)\s+-->/, window._lk_db={x:..,y:..,z:..} (concatenated x+y+z), /<div data-dpi="..">/, /<script nonce="([0-9a-zA-Z]+)">/, window._xy_ws='..'
Fetched with Referer header set to the site base URL

components.name: Master / decryption key feed
components.role: The other half of the secret. Rotated server-side; community projects auto-scrape it and republish to GitHub raw so downstream scrapers can pull it without re-deobfuscating the player each time.
examples: RapidCloud slice table: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cinemaxhq/keys/e1/key (a JSON array of [start,len] index pairs)

v2 passphrase: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/itzzzme/megacloud-keys/refs/heads/main/key.txt (live as of 2026-06; a 64-char hex string, e.g. 3709ad8892f413166b796a10c7fb86018bd1be1c7ae6f4d2cfc3fdc299cb3205)
v3 master key: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/yogesh-hacker/MegacloudKeys/refs/heads/main/keys.json -> key["mega"] (now HTTP 404 post-shutdown)

components.name: e1-player.min.js (obfuscated player bundle)
components.role: The original source of truth for key generation. Heavily obfuscated (control-flow flattening, frozen non-writable objects, char-code arrays, proxied funcs, later WASM). Static AST extractors parse it to recover the key; this is the layer that breaks on every rotation.
examples: https://megacloud.tv/js/player/a/prod/e1-player.min.js?v=<Date.now()> (cache-bust query)
Key construction patterns hunted by extractors: String.fromCharCode(...arr), array.join, reversed strings, indexed mapping, proxied function returns

components.name: RapidCloud OpenSSL 'Salted__' decryptor
components.role: Earliest decrypt path. CryptoJS-compatible AES-256-CBC where key+IV are derived from passphrase+salt via OpenSSL EVP_BytesToKey (MD5, 3 rounds). Implemented with Node crypto, not CryptoJS, in aniwatch.
examples: base64 decode -> bytes[0:8]='Salted__', salt=bytes[8:16], ciphertext=bytes[16:]
EvpKDF: d0=MD5(pass+salt); d1=MD5(d0+pass+salt); d2=MD5(d1+pass+salt); key=d0||d1 (32B), iv=d2 (16B)
crypto.createDecipheriv('aes-256-cbc', key, iv)

components.name: v2 CryptoJS decryptor
components.role: Simplest era. Plain CryptoJS.AES.decrypt(sources, key) with the 64-hex passphrase string (CryptoJS internally does the Salted__/EvpKDF), then JSON.parse the UTF-8 output.
examples: CryptoJS.AES.decrypt(encrypted, key).toString(CryptoJS.enc.Utf8) -> JSON array of {file,type:'hls'}

components.name: v3 custom 3-layer cipher (decryptSrc2/keygen2)
components.role: Latest, NOT AES at all. keygen2(megacloudKey, clientKey) builds a derived key via a 31-multiplier rolling hash, XOR with 247, rotate, interleave with reversed clientKey, mod-95 into printable ASCII. decryptSrc2 then reverses 3 layers of: LCG-seeded (seed = hash; seed=seed*1103515245+12345 & 0x7fffffff) Caesar shift over a 95-char printable alphabet, columnar transposition, and a seeded-shuffle substitution. First 4 chars of output are the payload length.
examples: genKey = keygen2(megacloudKey, clientKey); decSrc = atob(src)
per layer: charShift (mod 95) + columnarCipher2(decSrc, layerKey) + inverse seedShuffle2 substitution; layers iterated 3..1
dataLen = parseInt(decSrc.substring(0,4),10); return decSrc.substring(4, 4+dataLen)

components.name: megaplay.buzz fallback
components.role: When MegaCloud itself is unsolvable, aniwatch scrapes an alternate mirror that returns plaintext sources (no crypto), trading reliability for an extra hop.
examples: GET https://megaplay.buzz/stream/getSources?id=<id>&id=<id> with full browser-like headers + Referer https://megaplay.buzz/stream/s-2/.../sub
data-id read from #megaplay-player in the iframe

components.name: consumet remote black-box decryptor
components.role: consumet.ts no longer does any crypto. Its MegaCloud extractor POSTs the raw embed URL to a third-party service and reads back already-decrypted {sources,tracks}. Centralizes the maintenance burden onto one opaque endpoint (and a single point of failure / trust).
examples: GET https://crawlr.cc/9D7F1B3E8?url=<encodeURIComponent(embedUrl)> -> {sources:[{url,quality}], tracks:[{file,label,kind}]} (from @consumet/extensions 1.8.8 dist/extractors/megacloud.js)
Earlier consumet eras used the cinemaxhq/keys slice-table approach in-process

howItWorks: FLOW (v3, the last working MegaCloud era), concrete steps:
1. From the chosen server you already have the embed iframe URL, e.g. https://megacloud.blog/embed-2/v3/e-1/<xrax>?k=1. Parse the xrax/sourceId with regex /\/([^\/\?]+)\?/.

2. GET the embed HTML: https://megacloud.blog/embed-2/v3/e-1/<xrax> with header Referer: <siteBase>/. Run the regex battery to scrape the per-request clientKey (nonce) — it may be in a <meta name="_gg_fb">, an HTML comment _is_th:, window._lk_db {x,y,z} (concatenate), a data-dpi attribute, a <script nonce="..">, or window._xy_ws.

3. Fetch the rotating master key: GET https://raw.githubusercontent.com/yogesh-hacker/MegacloudKeys/.../keys.json and read key['mega'] (in the original design you would instead deobfuscate e1-player.min.js to recover it).

4. Call the chokepoint: GET https://megacloud.blog/embed-2/v3/e-1/getSources?id=<xrax>&_k=<clientKey>. Response JSON: {sources:<base64>, encrypted:true, tracks, intro, outro}. If encrypted is false, sources is already plaintext — done.

5. Derive the working key: genKey = keygen2(megacloudKey, clientKey) (rolling hash *31, XOR 247, rotate by pivot, interleave with reversed clientKey, map mod-95 to printable ASCII, length 96 + lHash%33).

6. Decrypt: decSrc = base64decode(sources); then for layer = 3,2,1 reverse (a) an LCG-seeded Caesar shift over the 95-char printable alphabet, (b) a columnar transposition keyed by genKey+layer, (c) a seeded-shuffle substitution. Read length from decSrc[0:4], payload = decSrc[4:4+len].
7. JSON.parse(payload) -> [{file:'https://.../master.m3u8', type:'hls'}]. That .m3u8 is the raw HLS master playlist. tracks[] give subtitle .vtt URLs; intro/outro give skip timestamps.

8. The .m3u8 (and its .ts/.key segments) is host-locked by Referer/Origin checks, so you still need an m3u8 proxy to add Referer https://megacloud.blog/ before a browser <video>/hls.js can play it (separate layer).
EARLIER ERAS (for context):
v2: skip steps 2/5/6 — just GET .../v2/e-1/getSources?id=<id>, then CryptoJS.AES.decrypt(sources, key64hex).toString(Utf8) -> JSON.

RapidCloud slice-key era: GET .../ajax/e-1/getSources?id=<id>; fetch the [index,length] table from cinemaxhq/keys; walk the table to CUT key characters out of the ciphertext string itself (extractedKey += sources[i]; blank those positions), leaving the trimmed remainder as the actual ciphertext; then CryptoJS.AES.decrypt(trimmed, extractedKey).
Salted__ era: base64 decode, confirm bytes 0-7 == 'Salted__', salt = bytes[8:16], run OpenSSL EvpKDF (MD5, 3 iterations) to get 32-byte key + 16-byte IV, AES-256-CBC decrypt bytes[16:].

keyRepos.name: ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch (npm: aniwatch)
keyRepos.whatItProvides: The reference TypeScript extractor (src/extractors/megacloud.ts). Implements ALL eras: getMegaCloudClientKey (nonce scraper), keygen2/decryptSrc2 (v3 custom cipher), CryptoJS v2 path, RapidCloud Salted__/EvpKDF + slice-key path, and megaplay.buzz fallback. GitHub repo is DMCA-blocked (HTTP 451); the logic survives in the published tarball (v2.27.9 inspected).

keyRepos.name: ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch-api (npm: aniwatch is the lib; api is the server)
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Self-hostable Express HTTP API serving hianime data using the aniwatch lib. Issue #30 there is the documented origin of the matchingKey() regex used to pull key values out of the player script.

keyRepos.name: consumet.ts / @consumet/extensions
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Multi-provider scraper lib. Its current MegaCloud extractor (dist/extractors/megacloud.js, v1.8.8) outsources decryption entirely to https://crawlr.cc/9D7F1B3E8?url=... and returns decrypted sources. Demonstrates the 'give up and proxy to a black box' strategy.

keyRepos.name: itzzzme/megacloud-keys
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Community auto-updated v2 master passphrase (64-char hex). Still resolving as of 2026-06-21. Lets scrapers decrypt without deobfuscating the player themselves.

keyRepos.name: yogesh-hacker/MegacloudKeys
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Community v3 master key feed exposing key['mega']. Now returns HTTP 404 (went dark around the 2026 shutdown) — illustrates the fragility of depending on a third party's scraped key.

keyRepos.name: cinemaxhq/keys
keyRepos.whatItProvides: The RapidCloud-era slice-position table ([index,length] pairs) used to carve the key out of the ciphertext. Referenced by both consumet and aniwatch's RapidCloud path.

keyRepos.name: Eggwite/megacloud-key-extractor
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Standalone Babel-AST deobfuscator that statically extracts the AES key from e1-player.min.js (handles char-code arrays via emulated String.fromCharCode, array.join, reversed strings, proxied funcs). Explicitly marked NO LONGER FUNCTIONAL — concrete proof the static approach loses the arms race.

technologies: AES-256-CBC (the canonical MegaCloud/RapidCloud cipher in v1/v2 eras)
CryptoJS (brix/crypto-js) — does Salted__/EvpKDF transparently; used by v2 and consumet RapidCloud paths
OpenSSL 'Salted__' format + EVP_BytesToKey (EvpKDF): MD5, 3 iterations, key=d0||d1, iv=d2
Node.js crypto (createHash('md5'), createDecipheriv('aes-256-cbc')) — aniwatch's hand-rolled EvpKDF
Custom bespoke cipher (v3): LCG PRNG (seed*1103515245+12345 & 0x7fffffff), 95-char printable-ASCII Caesar substitution, columnar transposition, seeded Fisher-Yates shuffle, BigInt rolling hash *31
Babel AST traversal for static key extraction from obfuscated player JS
WASM (latest player builds moved key-gen into WebAssembly to defeat AST extractors)
cheerio (HTML parsing of the embed iframe to find data-id / nonce)
axios / fetch with spoofed headers: X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest, Referer, browser User-Agent
HLS / .m3u8 master playlists (the ultimate payload) + AES-128 segment keys at the playback layer
gotchas: The crypto is a moving target by design: every key rotation or obfuscation change breaks every static extractor. Eggwite's AST extractor and most forks are already dead; expect to re-RE the player after each MegaCloud deploy.

Two fundamentally different 'keys' across eras: a string passphrase (v2) vs. a [index,length] slice table that CARVES the key out of the ciphertext (RapidCloud) vs. master-key + per-request nonce fed to a custom cipher (v3). Copy-pasting a v2 decryptor onto v3 data silently produces garbage.

v3 is NOT AES — decryptSrc2 is a bespoke layered substitution/transposition. Anyone expecting CryptoJS.AES.decrypt to work on v3 'sources' will fail; you must port keygen2 + the 3-layer reverse exactly (BigInt arithmetic, exact LCG constants, 95-char alphabet starting at char 32).
getSources returns {encrypted:false} sometimes (depends on the embed/server variant) — code must branch and NOT attempt decryption, or it throws on plaintext.

Depending on community key repos (itzzzme, yogesh-hacker) outsources your single point of failure to a stranger's GitHub: yogesh-hacker/keys.json is already 404. consumet's crawlr.cc dependency is worse — an opaque third party that can log your traffic, rate-limit, inject, or vanish.

The recovered .m3u8 is Referer/Origin-locked (403 without Referer: https://megacloud.blog/), so extraction alone is useless for playback — you still need an m3u8 proxy that rewrites segment URLs and injects headers. Treat extraction and proxying as two coupled layers.

Nonce hiding spot rotates: the v3 scraper carries 6+ regexes because MegaCloud kept moving clientKey between a meta tag, HTML comment, window._lk_db, data-dpi attr, script nonce, and window._xy_ws. A scraper that hardcodes one location dies on the next build.

Header fingerprinting: getSources rejects requests lacking X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest and a correct Referer; megaplay.buzz fallback needs a near-complete browser header set (Sec-Fetch-*, Accept-Language, etc.).

Legal: the canonical GitHub repos are DMCA-blocked (HTTP 451) and hianime itself shut down ~Mar-Jun 2026. Building on this stack means building on infrastructure that is actively being taken down — the case for a legit/self-hosted or licensed-aggregator design.

EvpKDF subtlety: CryptoJS uses MD5 with NO iteration count and salt-from-Salted__; reimplementing in another language requires matching the exact d_i = MD5(d_{i-1} || pass || salt) chain or key/iv will be wrong with no error until the final UTF-8 decode fails.

refuted.claim: 6. aniwatch's GitHub repo and Eggwite/megacloud-key-extractor returned HTTP 451 (DMCA); details reconstructed from npm tarball + search snippets, not live source repo.
refuted.note: Only HALF true. github.com/ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch = CONFIRMED HTTP 451 (Unavailable For Legal Reasons). BUT github.com/Eggwite/megacloud-key-extractor is NOT 451 — it is fully accessible (loaded fine), just ARCHIVED/read-only since 2026-06-08 and the README says it is 'NO LONGER FUNCTIONAL'. So the assertion that Eggwite returned 451 is false. The general point (the aniwatch source repo is DMCA-blocked, forcing reliance on the npm tarball) stands for aniwatch only.

refuted.claim: 7. Whether MegaCloud's latest key-gen is fully WASM-obfuscated is reported but not directly verified (artifact uses a scraped master key, sidestepping the player).
refuted.note: Reported public reverse-engineering (Eggwite blog, dated 2025-06-20) states the OPPOSITE of 'latest = WASM': MegaCloud's key-gen WAS WASM for a long time, then 'the WASM was gone, replaced by a sprawling mess of obfuscated JavaScript' ('The Great Upheaval'). So the latest key-gen is reportedly heavily-obfuscated JS (control-flow flattening, computed props, IIFEs), with WASM being the earlier era. The claim's hedge ('not directly verified in the inspected artifact') is fair because the inspected artifacts (community key feed + crawlr.cc proxy) deliberately sidestep the player — but framing 'fully WASM' as the current/latest state is outdated/contradicted by current sources.

confirmed: 1. itzzzme/megacloud-keys/key.txt was live and returned a 64-char hex key (3709ad8892...cb3205) on 2026-06-21; rotates frequently; repo may be taken down at any time.
2. yogesh-hacker/MegacloudKeys/keys.json returned HTTP 404 on 2026-06-21 (was the v3 'mega' key source) — likely permanently gone post-shutdown.

3. consumet @consumet/extensions 1.8.8 routes MegaCloud decryption through https://crawlr.cc/9D7F1B3E8 — third-party endpoint that can change/rate-limit/disappear; treat as unverified beyond the artifact.
5. Exact v3 cipher constants (XOR 247, hash mult 31, shift 5, LCG 1103515245/12345, key length 96 + lHash%33) are from aniwatch 2.27.9's bundled build; a later build could tweak them.
corrections: Claim 6 is materially wrong: Eggwite/megacloud-key-extractor did NOT return 451 — it is publicly accessible, archived (read-only) since 2026-06-08, with a README that says 'NO LONGER FUNCTIONAL'. Only ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch returned HTTP 451. Do not assert both were DMCA-blocked.

Claim 7 has the direction backwards relative to current reporting: the WASM key-gen is the OLDER era; per Eggwite's analysis MegaCloud REMOVED the WASM and replaced it with heavily-obfuscated JavaScript ('The Great Upheaval'). The latest key-gen is reported as obfuscated JS, not 'fully WASM'.

Claim 4 mislabels megacloud.tv as a legacy/possibly-defunct domain. In reality megacloud.blog (the v3 host) expired/went down and traffic migrated TO megacloud.tv (the .blog->.tv switch, ~April 2026 per RAELIE1/MegaCloudFix). megacloud.tv is the newer active host, not the dead one.

Claim 4's 'rapid-cloud.co may be defunct' is unsupported as stated: updownradar reported rapid-cloud.co UP (HTTP 200) in June 2026, conflicting with an earlier 'suspended Aug 2025' signal. Status is genuinely ambiguous, not confirmed-defunct.

Nuance on claim 2: the keys.json file path 404s, but the parent repo (github.com/yogesh-hacker/MegacloudKeys) returns 451/DMCA, not 404 — so 'permanently gone post-shutdown' is better characterized as DMCA-blocked at the repo level, which strengthens (not weakens) the claim.
additionalFindings: Exact crawlr.cc usage in @consumet/extensions@1.8.8 dist/extractors/megacloud.js: `const apiUrl = 'https://crawlr.cc/9D7F1B3E8?url=' + encodeURIComponent(videoUrl.href); const { data } = await this.client.get(apiUrl);` — confirms consumet fully delegates decryption (gets back already-decrypted sources). Note consumet 1.8.8 ships providers/anime/hianime.js (NOT a 'zoro.js' file); the zoro provider name is legacy and the file is hianime.js in this build.

aniwatch 2.27.9 bundles everything into a single dist/index.js (~85 KB) — there is no separate dist/extractors/megacloud.js in the published package, so verification must be done against the bundled index.js (the megacloud.ts source lives only in the now-451 GitHub repo).

Domain timeline corroboration: HiAnime shut down ~2026-03-13 after an ACE $18.75M judgment and inclusion in the 2025 Notorious Markets list (CBR / agence404 reporting). This is consistent with the 'post-shutdown key repos going dark' narrative.

Eggwite blog (eggwite.moe/blog/megacloud-key-extraction-analysis, dated 2025-06-20) is the strongest available primary source on the WASM->obfuscated-JS transition and on control-flow-flattening deobfuscation via Babel; it pre-dates the 2026-03 shutdown, so 'latest' state for key-gen is JS-obfuscation as of mid-2025 onward.

Could not retrieve exact npm publish timestamps for @consumet/extensions 1.8.8 vs 1.8.7 (registry response was truncated) and Cloudflare Radar for rapid-cloud.co was 403/forbidden — these specific data points remain unverified. The case study contains NO dollar/CPM/cost figures to fact-check (the only money figure surfaced is the unrelated ACE $18.75M anti-piracy judgment, which checks out across sources).

Eggwite/megacloud-key-extractor README itself now recommends 'finding a Crypto-JS patch alternative' and warns the obfuscated script structure changed significantly — independent corroboration that AST/pattern-based key extraction is broken and that the surviving approaches are (a) community key feeds [aniwatch path] and (b) remote proxy decryptors [consumet/crawlr.cc path], exactly as the overview describes.

layer: Player & client-side HLS playback (loading the proxied HLS master playlist into a web player, level/quality switching, sub/dub + multi-server fallback UX, WebVTT subtitles, thumbnail seeking, skip intro/outro, autoplay-next, continue-watching).
overview: This is the browser-side layer that takes the proxied .m3u8 URL (produced by the scraping API + m3u8/CORS proxy upstream) and turns it into an actual playing video with the full "anime site" UX: a quality menu, a sub/dub toggle, a server list (hd-1/hd-2 fallback), soft-subs (WebVTT), a seek-bar thumbnail strip, skip-intro/skip-outro buttons, autoplay-next, and continue-watching. The core decision is hls.js (Media Source Extensions) for Chrome/Firefox/Edge vs native HLS in the <video> tag for Safari/iOS — every serious site feature-detects and uses both. On top of that you pick a player UI shell: Artplayer is by far the most common in the zoro/hianime-clone ecosystem (it has a built-in customType hook, settings/quality/subtitle selectors, and an official VTT-thumbnail plugin), with Vidstack as the modern React-friendly alternative, Plyr as a lightweight but HLS-quality-limited option, and video.js as the heavyweight enterprise choice. The intro/outro skip data comes from two sources: the MegaCloud/aniwatch API itself returns intro{start,end} and outro{start,end} per episode, and/or the free aniskip.com v2 API keyed by MAL id. Crucially, because the segments are served through a CORS proxy that injects the upstream Referer/Origin headers, the player itself needs almost no special config — hls.js just fetches the already-rewritten, already-CORS-enabled playlist. The only real proxy-specific hls.js tuning is around retries/timeouts/buffer because proxied segments are slower and flakier than a first-party CDN.

components.name: hls.js (MSE engine)
components.role: JavaScript HLS implementation using Media Source Extensions; transmuxes MPEG-2 TS / fMP4 segments and feeds them to a <video> element on browsers without native HLS (Chrome, Firefox, Edge <142, all desktop except Safari). Provides the ABR ladder (hls.levels), manual level switching (hls.currentLevel), subtitle tracks, and the xhrSetup/loader hooks. This is the actual playback brain; the UI players just wrap it.
examples: new Hls({ maxBufferLength: 30, backBufferLength: 90, fragLoadPolicy retries }); hls.loadSource(proxiedMasterUrl); hls.attachMedia(video)
Hls.isSupported() feature-detect; on Hls.Events.MANIFEST_PARSED read hls.levels[] to build the quality menu
hls.currentLevel = idx to switch quality; hls.currentLevel = -1 for AUTO/ABR

components.name: Native Safari / iOS HLS
components.role: On Safari (macOS) and all iOS browsers (which lack the MediaSource API), you skip hls.js entirely and set video.src = proxiedM3u8 because Apple bakes HLS decoding into the engine. Feature-detect with video.canPlayType('application/vnd.apple.mpegurl'). Quality switching and subtitle selection are then handled by Safari natively, which limits how much custom UI you can impose.
examples: if (video.canPlayType('application/vnd.apple.mpegurl')) { video.src = url }
iOS Safari has NO MediaSource → hls.js cannot run there → native path is mandatory, not optional
Modern Safari ManagedMediaSource (MMS) is a partial middle ground but most clones still use plain native src on Apple devices

components.name: Artplayer (artplayer.js)
components.role: The dominant player UI shell in the zoro/hianime-clone ecosystem. Plain-JS, framework-agnostic, ~MIT. Exposes a customType.m3u8 hook where you instantiate hls.js, plus built-in settings panel, quality selector, subtitle selector (.vtt/.srt/.ass), Chromecast, and an official artplayer-plugin-vtt-thumbnail for seek-bar previews. Used by AniTeams, voidanime, and most public clones.
examples: customType: { m3u8: function(video,url,art){ if(Hls.isSupported()){ const hls=new Hls(); hls.loadSource(url); hls.attachMedia(video); art.hls=hls; } else if(video.canPlayType('application/vnd.apple.mpegurl')){ video.src=url } }
quality: hls.levels.map(l=>({html:l.height+'p', value:i})), onSelect switches hls.currentLevel
plugins: [artplayerPluginVttThumbnail({ vtt: thumbnailVttUrl })]; subtitle:{url, type:'vtt'} + settings subtitle selector for multi-lang

components.name: Vidstack Player
components.role: Modern Web-Components/React player, actively used by newer clones ('Vidstack player with skip intro and outro'). Defaults to loading hls.js from jsDelivr (dev = full bundle, prod = minified), configurable via the `config` prop and a `library` override. Fires onHlsLibLoaded/onProviderChange so you can grab the raw hls instance to set xhrSetup. Has first-class VTT thumbnail storyboard support and a built-in time-slider preview.
examples: <media-provider> config={{ xhrSetup: (xhr,url)=>{...} }}
on provider-change: if (provider?.type==='hls') provider.config = {...}; provider.instance is the raw Hls()
Thumbnails: JSON/VTT/storyboard ({url,tileWidth,tileHeight,tiles[{startTime,x,y}]}) bound to the slider preview

components.name: Plyr
components.role: Lightweight (~5MB unminified pkg, far smaller gzipped) accessible player, ~29.8k stars. Popular but has a known gap: NO native HLS quality switching — you must manually bridge hls.levels into Plyr's quality menu on MANIFEST_PARSED and call hls.currentLevel yourself. Good for simple skins, weaker for the full multi-quality anime UX.
examples: On Hls.Events.MANIFEST_PARSED: build quality:{options:[0,...heights], forced:true, onChange:(h)=>hls.currentLevel=levelIndexForHeight}
Prepend 0 = 'Auto' to the quality list; single source URL, not per-quality URLs
Used in AniTeams' alternative plyr/ implementation

components.name: video.js
components.role: Heaviest, most mature player (~39.8k stars, large bundle); enterprise-grade plugin ecosystem (videojs-contrib-quality-levels, http-streaming). v10 beta is a ground-up rewrite (collab with Plyr/Vidstack/Media Chrome) with a Streaming Processor Framework claiming ~12% the size of hls.js-light for simple ABR. Overkill for most clones but chosen when you want a battle-tested skinning/plugin system.
examples: videojs-contrib-quality-levels + videojs-http-source-selector for the quality menu
@videojs/http-streaming (VHS) can play HLS without hls.js
Less common in scraper-clone repos than Artplayer

components.name: aniskip v2 API client
components.role: Free community API (https://api.aniskip.com/v2) returning crowd-sourced opening/ending timestamps keyed by MyAnimeList id. Used as the primary or fallback source of skip-intro/skip-outro intervals. Cached in localStorage by clones (e.g. streambert_aniskipCache). op/mixed-op map to 'intro', ed/mixed-ed map to 'outro'.
examples: GET https://api.aniskip.com/v2/skip-times/{malId}/{episodeNumber}?types=op&types=ed&episodeLength={sec}
Response: { found:true, results:[{ interval:{startTime:1321.01,endTime:1401.16}, skipType:'ed', skipId, episodeLength:1417.16 }], statusCode:200 }
skipTypes: op, ed, mixed-op, mixed-ed, recap

components.name: MegaCloud / aniwatch episode-sources API
components.role: The upstream that yields the playable data: sources[].url (.m3u8, isM3U8/type:'hls'), tracks[] (WebVTT subtitle files with label+kind:'captions' AND a kind:'thumbnails' VTT for seek previews), and per-episode intro{start,end} / outro{start,end} timestamps extracted from MegaCloud directly (used instead of/alongside aniskip). Servers hd-1/hd-2 (a.k.a. megacloud/vidstreaming variants) and category sub/dub/raw drive the server-list + sub/dub fallback UX.
examples: GET /api/v2/hianime/episode/sources?animeEpisodeId={id}&server=hd-1&category=sub

Returns { sources:[{url,type:'hls',isM3U8:true}], tracks:[{file,label:'English',kind:'captions',default:true},{file:'thumbnails.vtt',kind:'thumbnails'}], intro:{start,end}, outro:{start,end}, anilistID, malID }
Server fallback UX: try hd-1 → on error try hd-2; sub/dub toggle re-requests with category=dub

components.name: m3u8 / CORS proxy (consumed, not part of this layer but it shapes player config)
components.role: The reason the player needs almost zero special config. The proxy fetches the upstream playlist, injects required Referer/Origin/User-Agent headers, recursively rewrites segment/key/sub-playlist URLs to also go through the proxy, and adds Access-Control-Allow-Origin. hls.js then just loads the proxied master URL like a normal CORS-friendly stream. The player only adds retry/timeout tuning to absorb proxy flakiness.
examples: VITE_PROXY_URL=https://m8u3.vercel.app/m3u8-proxy?url= ; loadSource(PROXY + encodeURIComponent(rawM3u8))
gogoanime-and-hianime-proxy .../m3u8-proxy?url= and .../hls-proxy?url= patterns seen in clones
Manifest rewrite is recursive: segment URLs, EXT-X-KEY URI, and nested variant playlists all re-pointed at the proxy

howItWorks: 1) Client calls the scraping API (e.g. /api/v2/hianime/episode/sources?animeEpisodeId=...&server=hd-1&category=sub). It gets back sources[].url (a raw MegaCloud .m3u8), tracks[] (WebVTT subtitle files + a thumbnails VTT), and intro/outro {start,end} timestamps.
2) Client wraps the raw .m3u8 in the CORS/m3u8 proxy: PROXY + encodeURIComponent(rawUrl). The proxy is what makes the stream loadable cross-origin and injects the upstream Referer the CDN requires.

3) Feature-detect playback path: if Hls.isSupported() (Chrome/Firefox/Edge) → new Hls(config); hls.loadSource(proxiedUrl); hls.attachMedia(video). Else if video.canPlayType('application/vnd.apple.mpegurl') (Safari/iOS) → video.src = proxiedUrl and let Safari handle it natively. iOS has no MSE so this branch is mandatory.

4) On Hls.Events.MANIFEST_PARSED, read hls.levels[] (each has .height/.bitrate) and populate the player's quality menu. Selecting a quality sets hls.currentLevel = index; choosing AUTO sets hls.currentLevel = -1 to re-enable ABR. (Native Safari does ABR/quality itself.)

5) Subtitles: add each tracks[] WebVTT file as a soft-sub. In Artplayer use subtitle:{url,type:'vtt'} plus a settings selector that swaps the active VTT for multi-language; in hls.js multi-track manifests you can instead switch hls.subtitleTrack. Default track is the one with default:true.

6) Thumbnail seeking: the tracks[] entry with kind:'thumbnails' is a WebVTT file whose cues point to a sprite-sheet with #xywh=x,y,w,h fragments. Artplayer's artplayer-plugin-vtt-thumbnail or Vidstack's storyboard/thumbnail component renders the preview image as you scrub the time slider.

7) Skip intro/outro: prefer the API's intro/outro {start,end}; if absent, call aniskip GET /v2/skip-times/{malId}/{ep}?types=op&types=ed&episodeLength={sec} and map op→intro, ed→outro. During playback a timeupdate handler shows a 'Skip Intro/Outro' button while currentTime is inside the interval; clicking sets video.currentTime = interval.endTime. Results are cached in localStorage to avoid re-fetching.

8) Multi-server + sub/dub fallback UX: render a server list (hd-1, hd-2) and a sub/dub toggle. On a fatal hls.js error (Hls.Events.ERROR with fatal:true) or empty sources, automatically re-request the next server; the sub/dub toggle re-calls the sources endpoint with category=dub and reloads the same episode.

9) Autoplay-next: on the video 'ended' event (or near-outro), look up the next episode id in the episode list and navigate/reload the player with it; many clones gate this behind a user setting and show a countdown overlay.

10) Continue-watching persistence: a throttled timeupdate handler writes {episodeId, currentTime, duration, updatedAt} either to localStorage (anonymous, zero-cost, the default for most clones) or to a DB like MongoDB (voidanime) for cross-device sync when users log in. On load the player seeks to the saved currentTime if < ~95% watched.

keyRepos.name: video-dev/hls.js
keyRepos.whatItProvides: The MSE-based HLS playback engine every web clone depends on. ~16.7k stars. Exposes hls.levels, hls.currentLevel, hls.subtitleTrack, xhrSetup/loader hooks, Events.MANIFEST_PARSED/ERROR. API.md documents all buffer/retry config.

keyRepos.name: zhw2590582/ArtPlayer
keyRepos.whatItProvides: The most-used player UI shell in anime clones. customType.m3u8 hook for hls.js, built-in quality/subtitle settings panels, Chromecast, official plugins (artplayer-plugin-vtt-thumbnail, -hls-control, -multiple-subtitles).

keyRepos.name: vidstack/player
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Modern web-component/React player. Auto-loads hls.js from jsDelivr, config prop + library override, provider-change hook to access raw Hls instance for xhrSetup, first-class VTT thumbnail storyboards and skip-button-friendly layouts.

keyRepos.name: sampotts/plyr
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Lightweight accessible player (~29.8k stars). Note the well-documented HLS quality-switch gap (issues #1741/#1919/#652) requiring manual bridging of hls.levels into Plyr's quality menu.

keyRepos.name: ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch (+ aniwatch-api)
keyRepos.whatItProvides: The scraper package + Node API whose /episode/sources response defines the data the player consumes: sources[].url(.m3u8), tracks[] (captions + thumbnails VTT), intro/outro {start,end}, anilistID/malID, servers hd-1/hd-2, category sub/dub/raw. Several repos in this org were DMCA/HTTP-451 blocked in 2024-2026 (README returned 451 during this research) — rely on npm artifacts, forks, mirrors.

keyRepos.name: aniskip/aniskip-api
keyRepos.whatItProvides: The free crowd-sourced skip-times API (api.aniskip.com/v2). GET /v2/skip-times/{malId}/{ep}?types=op&types=ed&episodeLength=... returns results[].interval{startTime,endTime} + skipType. Powers skip-intro/outro when the streaming API lacks timestamps.

keyRepos.name: anime-kun32/AniTeams-amvstrm / aniteams-player
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Concrete reference clone showing Artplayer + (alt) Plyr wiring: applyProxy() wrapping m3u8, quality selector via art.switchUrl, Default/Backup server list, localStorage timestamp persistence. Good 'minimal viable player' example.

keyRepos.name: voidbornfr/voidanime
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Fuller clone (Artplayer + hls.js + Vidstack, Chromecast, multi-source fallback Aniwatch/Gogo/Hianime/AnimePahe, VITE_PROXY_URL m3u8 proxy fallbacks, MongoDB-backed continue-watching). Shows the DB-persistence path vs pure localStorage.

technologies: hls.js (MediaSource Extensions HLS engine)
Native HLS via <video> (Safari/iOS, application/vnd.apple.mpegurl)
Media Source Extensions (MSE) / ManagedMediaSource
Artplayer.js (+ artplayer-plugin-vtt-thumbnail, -hls-control)
Vidstack Player (web components / React)
Plyr
video.js (+ videojs-contrib-quality-levels, @videojs/http-streaming)
WebVTT (subtitle tracks + sprite-sheet thumbnail cues with #xywh)
aniskip v2 API (api.aniskip.com)
m3u8/CORS proxy (Vercel-hosted, Referer/Origin injection, recursive manifest rewrite)
localStorage (continue-watching, aniskip cache) / MongoDB (cross-device sync)
HTML5 <video> timeupdate/ended events
gotchas: iOS Safari has NO MediaSource API, so hls.js literally cannot run there — you MUST implement the native video.src branch or playback is fully broken on iPhone/iPad. This is the #1 mistake.

Quality switching is NOT free in every player: Plyr has no native HLS quality menu (issues #1741/#1919) — you must read hls.levels on MANIFEST_PARSED and set hls.currentLevel manually. Native Safari also won't expose your custom quality UI; Safari does its own ABR.

Because the proxy injects upstream Referer/Origin and rewrites every segment/key/sub-playlist URL, the player needs almost no special config — but if you forget to proxy ALL nested URLs (variant playlists, EXT-X-KEY URIs), AES-encrypted segments fail with cryptic decrypt errors.

Proxied streams are slow/flaky vs first-party CDNs — default hls.js retry/timeout settings cause frequent stalls. Bump fragLoadPolicy retries and timeouts; keep backBufferLength modest (e.g. 90) to limit memory on long episodes.

Two competing intro/outro sources: the MegaCloud API's own intro/outro vs aniskip. They can disagree or one can be empty. Prefer the API timestamps, fall back to aniskip by MAL id, and cache aniskip in localStorage (it's a free third-party API you can hammer otherwise).

Mixing sub and dub: a sub/dub toggle is a NEW sources request (category=dub) with a different .m3u8 AND different subtitle tracks — you must reload the source and re-attach subs, not just toggle a track.

Thumbnail VTT references sprite coordinates (thumbnails.jpg#xywh=...) loaded via native ; players like Vidstack can't easily inject auth/proxy headers on those img loads, so the thumbnail host must itself be CORS/hotlink friendly or proxied.
Autoplay-next is blocked by browser autoplay policies unless the player is muted or the user has interacted — gate it and expect it to silently fail on first load.
localStorage continue-watching is per-device and wiped on cache clear; only a DB (e.g. MongoDB as voidanime does) gives cross-device resume, at the cost of needing auth + backend storage.

Repos in this space get DMCA'd: ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch-api README returned HTTP 451 (Unavailable For Legal Reasons) during this research, and hianime itself shut down ~March-June 2026. Pin/vendor the npm packages and player libs you depend on rather than fetching at runtime from a CDN/repo that may vanish.

confirmed: Claim 1: hianime shut down ~March-June 2026 and several ecosystem repos were DMCA-blocked; the player layer (hls.js/Artplayer/Vidstack) is unaffected and reusable, but the aniwatch-api/MegaCloud sources endpoint shape may be dead/changed (post-shutdown unconfirmed).

Claim 2: ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch-api README returned HTTP 451; exact JSON field names (intro/outro, tracks[].kind) are reconstructed from snippets/clone code, not a freshly fetched README - treat field-level details as likely-but-unverified.

Claim 3: Native HLS support 'arrived in Chrome and Edge at version 142' per one 2025/2026 source - UNCONFIRMED against official release notes; even if true, shipping hls.js as fallback remains standard practice.
Claim 4: npm-compare bundle sizes (hls.js 'minified' in MB, Plyr ~5.33MB) are unpacked package sizes, not gzipped over-the-wire sizes; real gzipped hls.js is low-hundreds of KB.
Claim 5: video.js v10's 'Streaming Processor Framework ~12% the size of hls.js-light' is a beta vendor claim (videojs.org blog), not yet GA-verified.

Claim 6: Public clone proxy/API URLs (m8u3.vercel.app, gogoanime-and-hianime-proxy*.vercel.app, aniwatch-api-net.vercel.app) are community-hosted, ephemeral, may already be offline; illustrate patterns not stable endpoints.
corrections: Claim 3 is mislabeled 'UNCONFIRMED' - it is now confirmed/outdated-caution. Chrome 142 (stable ~Oct-Dec 2025) shipped native desktop <video> HLS playback, also in Edge 142 / Chromium desktop, corroborated by the hls.js maintainers' own discussion #7644. The 'one 2025/2026 source' framing understates how well-established this now is.

Claim 4's size figure needs tightening. 'Real gzipped hls.js is on the order of low-hundreds of KB' is too high for the GZIPPED size: gzipped full build is ~70 KB (light ~50 KB), i.e. low-tens of KB. 'Low-hundreds of KB' actually describes the MINIFIED-but-uncompressed size (~300-400 KB). The core point (MB-scale npm figures are unpacked, not over-the-wire) is correct; just state gzipped vs minified explicitly. Plyr over-the-wire is ~18 KB min+gzip, not the ~5.33MB unpacked figure.

Field-name nuance for Claim 2: the ghoshRitesh12 aniwatch-api shape (tracks[].file/label/kind='captions', intro{start,end}, outro{start,end}) is DIFFERENT from the Consumet/Zoro provider shape (sources[].url/quality/isM3U8, subtitles[].url/lang, intro{start,end}). The overview/researcher should not assume one schema across both upstreams - a frontend targeting both needs an adapter. Consumet's Zoro shape has no outro and uses subtitles[].lang rather than tracks[].label/kind.
additionalFindings: DMCA scope is broader than stated: the March 2026 GitHub takedown (Remove Your Media LLC for Crunchyroll/VIZ) named at least 8 repos - ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch, ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch-api, yogesh-hacker/MegacloudKeys, yahyaMomin/hianime-API, IrfanKhan66/hianime-mapper, Shalin-Shah-2002/Hianime_API, ayanrajpoot10/hianime-api, itzzzme/anime-api. The inclusion of MegacloudKeys is directly relevant to the playback layer: it is the decryption-key source for MegaCloud, so its removal threatens the entire sources/extractor pipeline upstream of the player, not just the API wrapper. Press also references a '900+ tool' wider takedown wave.

AniSkip dependency confirmed and stable: api.aniskip.com/v2 getSkipTimes is keyed by MAL id + episode number and returns interval{startTime,endTime}, skipType ('op','ed','mixed-op','mixed-ed','recap'), skipId and episodeLength. This is an independent, still-live fallback for skip-intro/outro that does NOT depend on the now-451'd aniwatch-api - a meaningful resilience point for a post-shutdown rebuild.

Artplayer claims in the overview all check out against the live repo (zhw2590582/ArtPlayer): customType hook (e.g. customHLS via hls.js), and official plugins artplayer-plugin-hls-control (quality), artplayer-plugin-vtt-thumbnail (seek-bar thumbnail strip), and artplayer-plugin-multiple-subtitles. Native .vtt/.ass/.srt support is built in. This substantiates why Artplayer dominates the zoro/hianime clone ecosystem.

No legitimate successor exists as of June 2026; opportunistic copycat domains (e.g. hianimetv.cfd appeared March 14, 2026) are unaffiliated. Operators hinted at a possible background 'recovery' but nothing confirmed. For a rebuild, the practical implication is that the upstream source/extractor target is a moving/uncertain target - reinforcing why the provider-agnostic player layer is the durable, reusable part and the sources adapter should be isolated behind an interface.

Practical caveat for Claim 3's native-HLS path: native <video> HLS on Chromium gives basic playback but does NOT expose hls.js-style level/quality selection, custom loader hooks for proxy retry/timeout tuning, or programmatic subtitle track injection - exactly the features the anime-site UX (quality menu, proxy retry tuning, soft-sub switching) depends on. So even with Chrome 142, hls.js remains the primary engine, not merely a Firefox fallback.

layer: Frontend application architecture & real open-source anime-streaming clones (clients on consumet / aniwatch-api)
overview: Free anime-streaming frontends are thin, mostly-static catalog UIs over two external services: a metadata source (AniList GraphQL and/or a Consumet/aniwatch-api scraper) and a per-episode HLS resolver that returns an .m3u8 URL plus subtitle tracks and intro/outro timestamps, which is then played in an HTML5 player (Vidstack, ArtPlayer, or Plyr) via hls.js. The frontend never hosts or transcodes video; it is a presentation layer over a scraper API and an m3u8 CORS/Referer proxy. Two dominant patterns appear in the real ecosystem: (1) a React + Vite SPA that talks straight to a Consumet/AniList backend with nearly everything client-rendered (Miruro is the canonical example, CC BY-NC license, React 18 + Vite 5 + styled-components + Vidstack); and (2) Next.js App Router apps that wrap the `aniwatch` npm scraper or a Consumet backend behind their own /app/api routes and use React Server Components + ISR for catalog pages and client components only for the player (AniTeams = Next 15, Airin = Next 14 + NextUI + Mongo/Redis, ErickLimaS/anime-website = Next 14 + Firebase + Redux). The route map (home, browse/genre, search, anime/[id], watch/[id]/[ep]) and component inventory (episode grid, server+quality switcher, player, recommendations, continue-watching) are stable and legitimately reusable; the scraper backend + m3u8 proxy are the volatile, legally-exposed layer that should be treated as a swappable, untrusted dependency. Note (2026): the upstream HiAnime source these clones scraped shut down ~March-June 2026 (Operation 404 / USTR notorious-market designation), and several upstream repos (ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch, ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch-api, consumet.ts docs) now return HTTP 451 to fetchers, so surviving knowledge comes from npm artifacts, forks, mirrors, and archived READMEs.

Vidstack (@vidstack/react) is the modern default; ArtPlayer (~55KB) is the lighter alternative sites migrate to for full DOM/z-index control; Plyr is a third toggle. All consume an HLS source loaded via hls.js (m3u8 must be proxied for CORS/Referer).

components.role: Centralized module that builds requests to the metadata backend, AniList GraphQL, and the m3u8 proxy, with caching and a CORS-proxy wrapper. In SPA builds it lives client-side; in Next builds it lives in /app/api route handlers or server actions.
examples: Miruro src/hooks/useApi.ts: fetchAnimeData/Info/Episodes/StreamingLinks/AdvancedSearch/Trending/Popular/TopAiring + env VITE_BACKEND_URL, VITE_PROXY_URL, VITE_API_KEY

howItWorks: DATA FLOW: User opens watch/[id]/[ep] -> frontend fetches anime metadata + episode list (AniList GraphQL or /api/v2/hianime/anime/{id} + .../episodes) -> resolves the chosen episode's sources (server + sub/dub) via the scraper backend -> backend returns an .m3u8 URL + VTT subtitle tracks + intro/outro times + required request headers (Referer/Origin) -> frontend rewrites the m3u8 URL to go through an m3u8 proxy (to inject Referer and add CORS) -> hls.js loads it into Vidstack/ArtPlayer.

TWO ARCHITECTURES: (A) React+Vite SPA (Miruro) calls a remote Consumet backend + AniList directly from the browser through VITE_PROXY_URL; everything is client-rendered, deployed as static assets on Vercel/Cloudflare Pages. (B) Next.js App Router (AniTeams/Airin/ErickLimaS) wraps the scraper in its own /app/api route handlers or server actions so secrets/headers stay server-side; catalog pages use RSC + ISR, only the player subtree is a client component.

ROUTE MAP (recommended, Next App Router): / (home rails, ISR ~1h) ; /browse or /catalog and /genre/[genre] (filterable grids, ISR ~6h) ; /search?q= (dynamic) ; /anime/[id] (details + episode grid, ISR ~1h with on-demand revalidate when new eps air) ; /watch/[id]/[ep] (mostly dynamic/client for the player; shell can be static) ; /api/* proxy routes (sources, m3u8 proxy) ; /callback (AniList/Firebase OAuth) ; /profile, /account ; not-found. The watch route param convention is /watch/[id]/[ep] or query form {slug}?ep={episodeId}.

CACHING / SSR STRATEGY: Catalog/metadata is slow-changing -> use ISR/SSG (Next: export const revalidate = 3600; if a route mixes fetches, the lowest revalidate wins, so isolate). Episode SOURCES are short-lived signed/tokenized HLS URLs -> never cache the m3u8; always resolve at request time (dynamic / client fetch). Use revalidateTag/revalidatePath on-demand when a new episode airs. Consumet/scraper backends rate-limit, so wrap them with LRU/Redis caching at your own /api layer (Airin uses Redis/Upstash; Miruro uses lru-cache).

PLAYER WIRING: Resolve sources -> set hls.js source to proxied m3u8 -> attach VTT subtitle tracks from the sources response -> fetch AniSkip skip-times by MAL id -> show OP/ED skip buttons when currentTime is inside an interval -> on ended, autoplay next episode and persist progress to localStorage (or DB/AniList). Quality levels come from the master m3u8 (hls.js levels); server/category switch triggers a fresh source resolve.

STATE MANAGEMENT: Deliberately minimal. Miruro uses React context (ThemeContext) + localStorage, no Redux/Zustand. AniTeams/Airin lean on RSC + URL/searchParams as state + Firebase/Mongo for user data. None of the maintained clones use React Query/SWR heavily; Apollo Client is used only for AniList GraphQL. Continue-watching and sub/dub preference are localStorage-keyed by anime id.

Zoro/HiAnime provider methods: search, fetchAnimeInfo, fetchEpisodeServers, fetchEpisodeSources(episodeId, server, subOrDub). StreamingServers enum includes VidCloud, VidStreaming, MegaCloud, StreamSB, StreamTape, GogoCDN, UpCloud. ISource/IVideo return url, quality, isM3U8, subtitles, intro/outro, headers. keyRepos.name: itzzzme/m3u8proxy (and Eltik/M3U8-Proxy, MetaHat/m3u8-streaming-proxy)
keyRepos.whatItProvides: The CORS/Referer m3u8 proxy layer the player needs: accepts an m3u8 URL + headers (e.g. Referer for MegaCloud), rewrites the playlist so each .ts segment is also proxied, and adds CORS. Deployable as a Cloudflare Worker or on Render. Without this the browser blocks the cross-origin, Referer-gated HLS stream.

keyRepos.name: zhw2590582/ArtPlayer + vidstack/player
keyRepos.whatItProvides: The two player options. Vidstack (@vidstack/react) = accessible React components/hooks, HLS via hls.js, default in Miruro/AniTeams. ArtPlayer = lighter (~55KB) full-featured HTML5 player with plugin HLS support that some sites migrate to for DOM/z-index control and custom skip-intro overlays. AniSkip (aniskip/aniskip-extension, api.aniskip.com/v2) supplies OP/ED timestamps.

technologies: Next.js 14/15 (App Router, RSC, ISR/SSG, route handlers, server actions)
React 18 + Vite 5 (SPA alternative)
TypeScript
Tailwind CSS / styled-components ^6 / NextUI / HeroUI
@vidstack/react player + hls.js (^1.5) ; ArtPlayer.js ; Plyr
AniList GraphQL via Apollo Client ^3.10 + graphql ^16
Consumet (@consumet/extensions) and aniwatch (^2.23) scraper libraries
aniwatch-api REST backend (Docker, port 4000)
m3u8 CORS/Referer proxy (Cloudflare Worker / Render)
AniSkip v2 API (api.aniskip.com) for intro/outro skip times
Firebase Auth + Firestore / MongoDB + Redis (Upstash) for auth, history, caching
axios ^1.6-1.8, swiper ^11, framer-motion ^12, lru-cache, nprogress, next-pwa
Vercel + Cloudflare Pages/Workers for hosting
gotchas: The frontend is the safe/portable part; the scraper backend + m3u8 proxy are what get DMCA'd. Architect so the metadata/source provider is a swappable interface (provider abstraction) and own only the UI + AniList metadata layer for a legal/self-hosted build.
Episode source URLs are short-lived, token/Referer-gated HLS endpoints - NEVER cache the .m3u8 or .ts via ISR/CDN. Resolve sources at request time (dynamic route / client fetch). Cache only metadata.

Browsers block the upstream HLS because it is cross-origin and Referer-gated (e.g. MegaCloud requires a Referer header). You MUST run an m3u8 proxy that injects headers and rewrites every .ts segment; a plain CORS proxy on the master playlist alone is insufficient.

Consumet/aniwatch-api are rate-limited and frequently break when the upstream site changes its HTML/JS obfuscation. Put your own /api layer with LRU/Redis caching and graceful fallback (multiple servers: hd-1, hd-2, megacloud) in front, and expect to update the scraper often.

SPA-only builds (Miruro pattern) leak the backend/proxy URL and API key into the browser bundle (VITE_* vars are public) and have weak SEO. Next.js route handlers / server actions keep secrets server-side and give SSG/ISR SEO for catalog pages - prefer the Next pattern for a real product.

If a route mixes fetches with different revalidate times, Next uses the LOWEST one for the whole route - isolate the dynamic source-resolution fetch from the cacheable metadata fetch or you lose all caching.
Long-running shows (One Piece 1000+ eps) require a virtualized/paginated episode grid; naive full-list rendering tanks performance.
Continue-watching in localStorage does not sync across devices - DB-backed (Airin/Mongo, ErickLimaS/Firebase) or AniList progress sync is needed for a real account feature.

Player migrations are common: Vidstack overlays have z-index/DOM-control pain for custom skip buttons; teams migrate to ArtPlayer for ~58% smaller bundle and full DOM control - budget for player churn.

Most reference clones are licensed CC BY-NC (Miruro) or are archived/read-only (AniTeams archived 2025-07-15) - cannot be used commercially as-is; treat them as architecture references, not drop-in code.

refuted.claim: Exact dependency versions (aniwatch ^2.23.3, @vidstack/react ^1.10.9, next ^15.1.6) are from current package.json snapshots and will drift.
refuted.note: Mixed - the 'will drift' prediction has already materialized. In aniteams/AniTeams package.json TODAY: next is ^15.1.6 (CONFIRMED exact), @vidstack/react is ^1.10.9 (CONFIRMED exact), but aniwatch is ^2.24.3, NOT ^2.23.3 (already drifted up). Separately, the aniwatch npm 'latest' dist-tag is now 2.27.9 (modified 2026-03-14, i.e., right at the shutdown), far ahead of any of these pins. So 2 of 3 named versions are still accurate in AniTeams, but the aniwatch pin is stale - exactly the drift the claim warned about.

confirmed: HiAnime/aniwatch shut down ~March-June 2026 (Operation 404 / FBI / USTR notorious-market) - reported by CBR/Beebom; exact date and whether it returns is unconfirmed and evolving.
ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch, aniwatch-api, and consumet.ts docs/source pages return HTTP 451 to web fetchers as of this research - availability via npm/forks/mirrors may change.
AniTeams marked archived/read-only 2025-07-15 and 'moved to aniteams/AniTeams' - the active fork location may change.
Miruro star count and any future migration from Vite to Next.js is unconfirmed; current repo is React+Vite.
AniSkip v2 endpoint shape (results[].interval.startTime/endTime, skipType) is from third-party docs/forks, not an official spec page - verify against api.aniskip.com before relying on it.
Public api.consumet.org being fully down vs partially up fluctuates; self-hosting is the documented requirement.
corrections: Claim 3 (versions): aniwatch is NOT ^2.23.3 in the current AniTeams package.json - it is ^2.24.3. The npm 'latest' dist-tag is 2.27.9 (published/modified 2026-03-14). The next ^15.1.6 and @vidstack/react ^1.10.9 pins are still exact in AniTeams as of this check.

Claim 2 (451): docs.consumet.org returns HTTP 200, not 451 - only the GitHub SOURCE repos (consumet.ts, aniwatch, aniwatch-api) are 451-blocked. Also, the aniwatch repos now live under the renamed account 'ritesshg' (formerly ghoshRitesh12); old URLs 301-redirect there before hitting the 451.

Claim 6 (AniSkip): the response shape IS backed by an official, live OpenAPI/Swagger spec self-served at api.aniskip.com/api-docs (typescript-aniskip-api 2.0.576) - so calling it 'not an official spec page' undersells it. The full skipType enum is op/ed/mixed-op/mixed-ed/recap (the claim only named startTime/endTime/skipType and omitted the enum values and the {statusCode, message, found} envelope).

Claim 1 (shutdown): the precise go-dark date is March 13, 2026 (coinciding with an $18.75M ACE judgment), not just a fuzzy 'March-June 2026' window. 'Operation 404' is most directly tied to 1xAnime/AnimeKai domain seizures; HiAnime's own takedown is attributed more to broad legal/ACE pressure than to Operation 404 specifically - the case-study should not conflate the two.
additionalFindings: Maintainer rename: ghoshRitesh12 renamed their GitHub account to 'ritesshg'. Any hardcoded links to github.com/ghoshRitesh12/* now 301 to github.com/ritesshg/* and then return 451. Mirrors/forks (e.g., the package.json still visible in search caches) are stale snapshots.

Resilience signal: despite the GitHub 451s, the supply chain is largely intact via npm - the aniwatch package remains fully published (65 versions, latest 2.27.9, modified 2026-03-14). A clone could still 'npm i aniwatch' even though the source repo is legally blocked. This validates the researcher's 'treat the scraper backend as a swappable, untrusted dependency' thesis.

aniwatch-api (the self-hostable Express wrapper, separate from the aniwatch npm lib) was last seen around v2.18.x in releases; it is the deployable HTTP-server counterpart to the library and is also 451-blocked on GitHub - self-hosters must rely on Docker images / forks / npm.

Post-HiAnime enforcement is broadening: CBR reports a follow-on MPA/CODA crackdown after the shutdown, 900+ piracy tools/GitHub-tool takedowns (Crunchyroll named in DMCA actions against scraper repos), and 9anime/AnimeKai also going dark - i.e., the 451 wave on scraper repos is expanding, so additional upstream repos beyond the three named may go dark.

AniSkip API version string observed is typescript-aniskip-api 2.0.576 - useful as a concrete pin if a clone wants to assert which spec revision it integrated against. The v2 GET also accepts an optional episodeLength query param (used to filter/scale results), which clones often omit.

Miruro license nuance: it is a Custom BY-NC license (not literally the standard Creative Commons CC BY-NC legal text) - functionally non-commercial-with-attribution, but a clone author relying on 'CC BY-NC' verbatim should read Miruro's actual LICENSE file since custom terms can differ from the canonical CC deed.

Consumet public-API decline is long-standing (PSA issue #486 dates the limiting to Sept 29, 2023), so '2025/2026 down' is the tail of a multi-year deprecation, not a new event - the documented path has been self-host/consumet.ts library for years, with Anify suggested for mappings.

layer: Solution architecture / product shape — every viable way to build an anime streaming product with the hianime UX, compared by legal risk, build effort, and cost
overview: There is no single "build an anime site" — there's a spectrum of seven distinct product shapes that all deliver a hianime-like browse/watch UX but differ enormously in legality, cost, and durability. They fall into three legal buckets: (1) clearly illegal piracy (A, B-with-scrapers); (2) legal-but-fragile gray/aggregation (D, E, parts of B that only proxy a third-party API); (3) fully legal and durable (C self-host BYO, E legal-embed, F licensing). The hianime case is the canonical (A): scrape source sites, decrypt MegaCloud/RapidCloud streams, proxy m3u8 through CORS workers, monetize with adult/popunder ad networks. Its open-source ecosystem (consumet.ts, ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch + aniwatch-api, MegacloudKeys, m3u8-proxy workers) is what made (A)/(B) cheap to clone — until the March 2026 Crunchyroll/VIZ-led DMCA §1201 anti-circumvention sweep took down 900+ repos and hianime itself went dark (~March–June 2026). The takedown is the key 2026 fact: the cheap pirate path now carries direct, demonstrated legal action against code hosts and operators, not just the streaming sites. For an indie dev who wants to ship something that survives, the realistic shapes are C (Jellyfin/Plex BYO skin), D (AniList-shaped tracker + JustWatch deep links), E (legal-embed aggregator of Muse/Ani-One YouTube + Tubi/Pluto FAST), and hybrids of those. Each is concretely cheap ($0–$50/mo) precisely because someone else (the user, the rights holder, or the AVOD platform) pays for storage, bandwidth, and licensing.

components.name: (A) Full pirate clone — self-host/proxy scraped streams
components.role: The literal hianime model. You run scrapers (consumet/aniwatch) against source CDNs (MegaCloud/megacloud.blog, RapidCloud), decrypt getSources with rotating CryptoJS keys, then EITHER hotlink the source CDN's m3u8 (cheap, fragile, leaks referer) OR proxy every video segment through your own infra (expensive but reliable). Catalog/metadata mapped to AniList/MAL. HIGHEST legal exposure: you are the infringing distributor. If you proxy segments you also pay full video bandwidth.
examples: Hotlink mode: cost stays near $0 because source CDN serves bytes, but breaks when source adds referer/token checks and exposes users to the source's malware ads
Proxy mode: ~$0.005–0.01/GB on Bunny Volume/Standard; a single 1080p episode ≈ 0.7–1.4 GB, so 1M episode-views/mo ≈ 700TB–1.4PB ≈ $3,500–$14,000/mo in bandwidth alone
Legal exposure: this is exactly what hianime did and got hit — USTR/MPA 'notorious market' listing, site dark ~2026
Time-to-MVP: 1–2 weeks from forks, but you sign up for permanent firefighting as MegaCloud rotates keys/HTML

components.name: (B) Thin clone — proxy someone else's aggregator API vs run your own scrapers
components.role: Two sub-shapes that look identical to users but differ in who maintains the scraper. (B1) THIN: your frontend calls a third-party hosted streaming API (consumet-hosted clones, ezvidapi, a friend's aniwatch-api deploy) and you never touch streams — lowest effort, but you depend on infra you don't control and that keeps dying. (B2) OWN SCRAPERS: you self-host consumet/aniwatch-api yourself (Node + Docker), giving control but inheriting the full maintenance + legal load of (A).
examples: B1 thin proxy: $0–$5/mo (just a Vercel/CF Pages frontend); but consumet's public API was shut down and self-host is now required, and hosted clones get DMCA'd — durability measured in months
B2 self-host aniwatch-api: ~$5–20/mo VPS; but providers 'break whenever upstream changes HTML, and you only find out in production'
Legal: B2 = same as (A) (you operate the scraper/decryptor = §1201 anti-circumvention target). B1 = you're a downstream infringer + your upstream is illegal, so it vanishes under you
The 414+ forks of aniwatch-api named in the 2026 DMCA show how thin these are and how en-masse they get removed

components.name: (C) Self-hosted BYO-files — Jellyfin/Plex skin, USER supplies files
components.role: You ship software (or a hosted control plane) where each user runs their own media server and provides their own video files (their rips, downloads via *arr stack, public-domain). You build the hianime-style UI/discovery layer on top of Jellyfin's API. The infringing copy, storage, and bandwidth all live on the USER's hardware — you host zero video. This is the durable, legitimate analog of hianime's UX.
examples: Cost to YOU: a metadata/UI app on a $5–20/mo VPS or even free static host; you serve no video bytes
Cost to USER: free Jellyfin software; storage box $400–$1,900 one-time or a $200–500 mini-PC; optional Usenet/indexer $5–20/mo
Stack: Jellyfin (free, FOSS) + Sonarr/Radarr/Bazarr/Jellyseerr (the *arr stack) + AniDB/AniList metadata agents
Legal: YOU are clean (general-purpose tool, à la VLC/Plex). Users' own copying is their exposure. This is the safest way to give power users a hianime feel

components.name: (D) Metadata / tracker / discovery only — AniList-shape, no video
components.role: Build a pure discovery + tracking product (lists, ratings, airing schedule, recommendations) with NO streams. Add legal 'where to watch' deep links by reading AniList's ExternalLinks / JustWatch-style availability. This is the AniList/MAL/Anilist-clone shape. Monetize via affiliate/referral to Crunchyroll/JustWatch, premium features, or non-adult display ads.
examples: Data: AniList GraphQL is free, no key, ~90 req/min, single endpoint https://graphql.anilist.co, 500k+ entries, includes ExternalLinkSourceCollection (Crunchyroll/Netflix/Hulu/VIZ links)
Cost: $0–$25/mo — just cache AniList responses; no video, no scraper, no DMCA surface
Legal exposure: effectively zero (facts/metadata + outbound links). This is what survives DMCA waves untouched
Time-to-MVP: days; it's a CRUD+GraphQL app. Right for devs who want a real, ownable product without legal risk

components.name: (E) Legal-aggregator — embed only official FREE sources
components.role: Aggregate and re-skin content that rights holders themselves give away free: Muse Asia & Ani-One Asia YouTube channels (official, licensed, embeddable via YouTube IFrame API), plus FAST/AVOD catalogs (Tubi, Pluto TV's HIDIVE channel, Crackle, Plex). You embed official players; the platform serves bytes and pays licensing. You get a hianime-like grid of genuinely legal, free anime.
examples: Muse Asia/Ani-One: official YouTube channels, daily simulcasts, embeddable — but geo-locked (Muse mostly SEA/India: Indonesia, Philippines, India, Vietnam, etc.)
FAST/AVOD: Pluto added 800+ hrs anime + 'Anime x HIDIVE' 24/7 channel (May 2025); Tubi 100M MAU (June 2025) absorbing ex-piracy users
Cost: $0–$50/mo (frontend + caching of public catalog metadata); zero video bandwidth, zero licensing

Legal: low IF you respect each platform's embed ToS (YouTube IFrame is fine; scraping Pluto/Tubi internal m3u8 is NOT — that crosses back into gray). Geo-gaps and limited catalog are the real product weaknesses

components.name: (F) Actually license catalog content
components.role: Become a real (even tiny) licensed AVOD/SVOD: pay minimum guarantees for non-exclusive streaming windows, host the files legally, monetize with ads or subscription. The only path to a fully-owned legal catalog, but capital-intensive and not an indie weekend project.
examples: Non-exclusive single-territory MG: $5,000–$50,000 per title; a 12-ep mid-tier season ~$10,000–$25,000 for a 2–3yr US window
Exclusive top-tier: $100,000–$500,000+/season; major-franchise US-exclusive simulcast >$150,000/episode; simuldub adds 15–30%
Plus DRM, CDN, encoding, and ad/sub ops on top — realistically six figures to launch a small legal catalog
Right for: funded startups or studios, not indies. An indie's 'F-lite' is creator/public-domain content (Creator-TV-style AVOD on Plex/Xumo)

components.name: (G) Hybrids — the pragmatic indie sweet spot
components.role: Combine the safe shapes so the legal/durable core carries the product and an optional power-user backend adds the 'just watch it here' magic without you hosting infringing streams. Best risk-adjusted options for a solo dev.
examples: D + C: AniList-shaped tracker (legal, the product) + optional 'connect your Jellyfin' so power users stream their own files in your UI — you host metadata only
E + C: legal-embed aggregator (Muse/Ani-One/Tubi) for the free catalog + BYO-Jellyfin fallback for everything not legally free
D + E: tracker with legal 'watch free now' embeds where available and deep links elsewhere — 100% legal, genuinely useful, ad/affiliate monetizable
Anti-pattern hybrid: D/E frontend + (A)/(B) scraper backend 'for convenience' — this re-imports ALL the legal risk; the 2026 takedown shows it's a when-not-if

howItWorks: RANK BY LEGAL RISK (highest→lowest): A and B2 (you operate scraper+decryptor = direct §1201 anti-circumvention + infringement target, demonstrated by 2026 sweep) > B1 (downstream infringer, dies when upstream dies) > E-if-you-scrape-FAST-m3u8 (ToS/CFAA gray) > D and E-done-right and C and F (legal). The 2026 Crunchyroll/VIZ action via Remove Your Media LLC cited 17 U.S.C. §1201(a)(2) against MegacloudKeys, aniwatch, aniwatch-api and 6+ named clones; GitHub found §1201 unproven but acted on 'other valid copyright claims' — meaning even the anti-circ defense didn't save the repos.

RANK BY BUILD EFFORT (lowest→highest): B1 thin proxy (days, fork a frontend) < D tracker (days, one GraphQL API) < E legal-embed (1–2 wks, per-platform integration + geo logic) < A/B2 pirate (1–2 wks to MVP but PERPETUAL maintenance as keys/HTML rotate) < C BYO (2–4 wks to build the Jellyfin-skin UX) << F licensing (months of deals + full streaming stack).

RANK BY INFRA COST ORDER-OF-MAGNITUDE (lowest→highest): D/E/B1 ≈ $0–$50/mo (no video bytes) < C ≈ $5–20/mo to you (user pays storage/bandwidth) < A/B2 hotlink ≈ $5–20/mo until source blocks you < A proxy-mode = $thousands–tens-of-thousands/mo at scale (700TB–1.4PB/mo for 1M episode-views at ~$0.005–0.01/GB Bunny) << F = six figures upfront (MGs) + ongoing CDN/DRM.

THE PIRATE STREAM PIPELINE (so you understand what A/B actually maintain): frontend → aggregator API (consumet/aniwatch-api) → scrape source HTML for episode→server map → call getSources (e.g. megacloud.blog/embed-2/v2/e-1/getSources?id=...) → decrypt the encrypted sources string with CryptoJS using keys pulled from an external repo (MegacloudKeys) → get raw .m3u8 → rewrite playlist + proxy segments through a CORS worker (Cloudflare Worker / Flask) to strip referer checks and add Access-Control headers. Every arrow is a breakpoint the source can change at will.

WHY CLOUDFLARE WORKERS ARE THE DEFAULT m3u8 PROXY (and the gotcha): Workers free tier = 100k req/day, R2 = zero egress fees, and Workers stream responses without buffering so no CPU-time limit on passthrough. BUT Cloudflare restricts serving video/large non-HTML files unless the bytes are hosted in a Cloudflare service (Stream/Images/R2) — the old §2.8 moved into CDN-specific Service Terms (2023). Proxying a third party's video through Workers at scale violates this and gets the account/route banned; that's why serious pirate proxies move to Bunny/own VPS.

WHO EACH SHAPE IS RIGHT FOR: A/B = nobody who wants to avoid lawsuits in 2026 (the model is now actively prosecuted and the supply chain is collapsing). C = devs serving power users / privacy crowd who already have files. D = devs wanting a real, ownable, monetizable product fast with ~zero legal risk. E = devs targeting markets where Muse/Ani-One/Tubi geo-coverage is good (SEA/India for Muse; US for FAST). F = funded teams only. G = the recommended default for an indie.

DECISION FRAMEWORK for an indie dev: (1) Will you host or proxy unlicensed video bytes? If yes → you are the pirate, stop unless you accept lawsuits + perpetual maintenance + ballooning bandwidth. (2) Want it legal AND durable AND cheap? → start with D (tracker) as the spine, it's the lowest-risk real product. (3) Want users to actually watch in your UI legally? → layer E (official YouTube/FAST embeds where licensed) + optional C (BYO Jellyfin) = the G hybrid. (4) Have capital and want a catalog you own? → F-lite with creator/public-domain content first, then small non-exclusive MGs. RECOMMENDATION: build D+E+optional-C (the safe hybrid). It gives ~80% of the hianime browse/discover/watch UX, costs <$50/mo, survives DMCA waves, and is monetizable via affiliate + non-adult display ads instead of the toxic popunder/adult networks that piracy depends on.
