keyRepos.name: DMCA notice (github/dmca 2026-03-23-crunchyroll)
keyRepos.whatItProvides: The primary-source 2026 takedown: Remove Your Media LLC for Crunchyroll/VIZ, §1201 claim, names aniwatch, aniwatch-api, MegacloudKeys, yahyaMomin/hianime-API, IrfanKhan66/hianime-mapper, ayanrajpoot10/hianime-api, itzzzme/anime-api and more. Read this before building (A)/(B).

technologies: Node.js + TypeScript (Cheerio + Axios) for scrapers
CryptoJS for MegaCloud/RapidCloud source decryption
HLS (.m3u8) + MPEG-TS segments; hls.js / video.js / Plyr / Vidstack players
Cloudflare Workers + R2 (zero-egress) for CORS m3u8 proxying
Bunny.net CDN/Stream/Volume Network for actual video bandwidth at $0.005–0.06/GB
Docker / Docker Compose for self-hosting aggregator APIs and the *arr stack
GraphQL (AniList) and REST (consumet) for metadata/source APIs
Jellyfin / Plex + Sonarr/Radarr/Bazarr/Jellyseerr (the *arr stack) for BYO
YouTube IFrame Player API for legal Muse Asia / Ani-One embeds
Next.js / Vercel / Cloudflare Pages for the frontend; Redis/KV for caching API responses
Ad/monetization: ExoClick, TrafficJunky, Adsterra, Clickadu, Monetag (piracy path); affiliate + non-adult display (legal path)
gotchas: The cheap pirate path is now actively litigated, not theoretical: the March 2026 Crunchyroll/VIZ sweep via Remove Your Media LLC removed 900+ repos AND hianime went dark (~March–June 2026). Building (A)/(B2) in 2026 means inheriting a model whose entire supply chain is collapsing.

DMCA §1201 anti-circumvention is the weapon, not ordinary copyright — distributing the decryption keys (MegacloudKeys) is itself the violation. Even though GitHub found §1201 'unproven,' it removed repos on 'other valid copyright claims,' so neither defense saved them.

Proxy-mode bandwidth is the silent budget killer: 1M 1080p episode-views/mo ≈ 700TB–1.4PB ≈ $3,500–$14,000/mo even on cheap Bunny; this is why pirate sites hotlink source CDNs and lean on toxic ad networks to survive.

Cloudflare Workers/CDN forbid serving video/large non-HTML bytes you don't host in CF (Stream/Images/R2) — old §2.8 moved into Service-Specific Terms (2023). A free-Workers m3u8 proxy works at toy scale and gets banned at real scale.

Scrapers are perpetual maintenance: consumet/aniwatch providers 'break whenever upstream changes HTML and you only find out in production'; MegaCloud rotates keys/endpoints frequently. You are signing up for unpaid on-call forever.

Thin-clone (B1) durability is illusory: consumet's public API was shut down, self-host is mandatory, and hosted aniwatch-api clones get DMCA'd in batches (414+ forks named at once). Your product vanishes when your upstream does.

Legal-embed (E) has two traps: (1) Muse Asia/Ani-One are geo-locked (mostly SEA/India), so coverage is thin for US/EU users; (2) scraping Tubi/Pluto/Plex INTERNAL m3u8 instead of using sanctioned embeds throws you back into ToS/CFAA gray territory — only the official YouTube IFrame embed is clearly safe.

Piracy monetization is structurally adult/popunder (ExoClick/TrafficJunky at ~$1.50 CPM Tier-1, ~$0.40 Asia) because mainstream networks (AdSense) ban infringing sites — that ad inventory is also the malware vector that harms your users and your reputation.
AniList rate limit (~90 req/min) means a (D) tracker MUST cache aggressively or you'll be throttled at modest traffic — cheap to fix with KV/Redis but easy to forget.

confirmed: hianime/aniwatch went offline ~March-June 2026 amid the anti-piracy push; some sources hint at a possible return and mirror domains (e.g. hianimez.to) keep appearing; domain/status is highly fluid and unconfirmed long-term.
The 'aniwatch' npm package is reportedly still published (v2.27.9, ~May 2026) despite the GitHub repo being 451-blocked; could be unpublished/deprecated at any time.
Bunny.net per-GB rates ($0.005 Volume / $0.01 EU-NA Standard, up to $0.06 Africa/ME) and Cloudflare R2 ($0.015/GB storage, $4.50/M Class A, zero egress) are current 2025-2026 pricing-page figures.
Pirate-ad CPMs (~$1.50 Tier-1, ~$0.40 Asia, ~$0.70 Africa, ~$1.00 CIS) are forum/blog benchmarks (BlackHatWorld/AdSpyGlass) and fluctuate.
Pluto's '800+ hours anime + Anime x HIDIVE channel (May 2025)' and 'Tubi 100M MAU (June 2025)' are platform-announcement figures likely already superseded.
The list of named DMCA-targeted repos is from the March 2026 notice; additional repos and forks have likely been added/removed since.
corrections: Claim 2 date error: aniwatch npm v2.27.9 was published 2026-03-14 (verified via registry.npmjs.org), NOT '~May 2026'. The publish date essentially coincides with the March 2026 DMCA wave, not two months after it.

Claim 3 (entries): '500k+ entries' must not be read as 500k anime. AniList's own docs give two different figures - the older GitBook says 'over 500k anime AND manga entries' (combined media), while current docs.anilist.co says 'over 20 thousand anime entries and over 100 thousand manga entries.' For an anime-site context the relevant number is ~20k anime, not 500k.

Claim 3 (rate limit): citing '~90 req/min' alone is misleading. AniList is currently running in a DEGRADED state capped at 30 req/min (documented as 'temporary' but long-standing). Architecture sizing should assume ~30/min, not 90/min.

Claim 5: the '15-30% simuldub premium' figure was not corroborated; sources instead describe a 30-50%+ SIMULCAST (not dub) premium over delayed licenses, exceeding 100% for hot seasonal titles. The '>$150k/ep' figure is at the TOP of the cited $50k-$200k/ep top-tier range, not a typical floor. Vitrina is a vendor/marketing source - flag accordingly.

Claim 6 source attribution: the cited CPMs ($1.50/$0.40/$0.70/$1.00) match Kadam's published popunder benchmarks almost exactly; BlackHatWorld/AdSpyGlass were not the closest verifiable source. Also note adult-traffic CPMs (relevant to pirate-anime monetization) run materially higher ($2-$8 on Adsterra).

Overview correction: the overview says the March 2026 sweep 'took down 900+ repos' and lists consumet.ts as part of the cheap-clone ecosystem. consumet/consumet.ts and consumet/api.consumet.org were NOT named in the 2026-03-23 Crunchyroll notice, yet both now return HTTP 451 - so consumet was taken down in a LATER/SEPARATE action, not the original notice. The 900+ figure (~939 incl. forks) is correct only for the original notice's scope.
additionalFindings: LIVE STATUS (verified via HTTP probe, June 2026): github.com/consumet/consumet.ts -> 451; github.com/consumet/api.consumet.org -> 451; consumet ORG page (github.com/consumet) -> 200 (org survives, key repos gone). This contradicts search-snippet claims that consumet repos were 'still active' - they are not.

The ghoshRitesh12 account renamed to 'ritesshg': github.com/ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch and /aniwatch-api now 301-redirect to github.com/ritesshg/aniwatch[-api], both still returning 451. So the code is gone from GitHub regardless of the rename - but the npm artifact (aniwatch@2.27.9) remains installable, confirming the claim-2 thesis that the package outlived the repo.

GitHub explicitly REJECTED the DMCA Sec 1201 anti-circumvention basis of the Crunchyroll/Remove Your Media notice (per TorrentFreak/Gigazine), removing repos only because the notice 'contains other valid copyright claim(s).' Architecturally significant: the theory that scraper/decrypt code is per-se illegal circumvention was NOT validated by GitHub - takedowns rested on ordinary copyright grounds. The overview's framing of a 'DMCA Sec 1201 anti-circumvention sweep' overstates the settled legal posture.

Cloudflare R2 detail not in claim 4: R2 has an Infrequent Access tier ($0.01/GB-month storage, $9.00/M Class A, $0.90/M Class B) and Class B is $0.36/M on Standard - useful for cost modeling a self-host/aggregator architecture.

Bunny.net Volume tier scales below the cited $0.005: $0.004/GB (500TB-1PB) and $0.002/GB (1PB-2PB). For high-volume video the effective floor is $0.002/GB, materially cheaper than the single $0.005 figure cited.
HiAnime scale context for the case study: ~244.2M visits in Aug 2025, ~17.5M unique visitors, global rank ~159 before shutdown - underscores why it was the 'canonical (A)' target.

Caveat on all piracy-site/domain claims: inherently volatile. As of the research date several 'hianime' clone domains are flagged as malware/phishing (hianime.dk/.se/.cv), so an architecture overview should not treat current mirror domains as stable or safe references.

layer: Real costs to build and operate a free anime streaming site, modeled at scale tiers (MVP/dev, ~10k, ~100k, ~1M, ~10M MAU), with bandwidth as the dominant, business-killing cost center.
overview: Free anime streaming economics are dominated by ONE line item: video egress bandwidth. Everything else (compute, storage, scrapers, DB, domains, Cloudflare) is rounding error once you have real traffic. Two architectures differ by ~100x in cost. (A) EMBED/AGGREGATOR (what zoro.to -> aniwatch -> hianime actually did): scrape the source, extract the MegaCloud/RapidCloud m3u8, and either iframe-embed the host's player (the THIRD PARTY pays the bandwidth, your cost ~$0) or proxy only the playlist+segments. The moment you run an m3u8/TS proxy to bypass CORS/Referer, EVERY video byte transits YOUR server and you inherit the full bandwidth bill (the 'proxy blow-up'). This is THE reason piracy sites embed instead of self-host. (B) SELF-HOST: you store and serve encoded video; bandwidth is enormous unless you exploit egress-free paths (Cloudflare R2, Backblaze B2 via Bandwidth Alliance, bunny.net Volume). Concrete anchor: a 24-min anime episode at 1080p HLS (~6.5 Mbps) is ~120 MB streamed (official 1080p downloads ~380 MB; ABR streaming rarely exceeds 200 MB/episode). A moderately engaged user watching ~30 episodes/month = ~3.6 GB/user/month. That number times your egress price IS your business. At 1M MAU x 3.6 GB = 3.6 PB/month. On AWS CloudFront ($0.085/GB) that's ~$306,000/mo (bankrupt); on bunny.net Volume ($0.005/GB) ~$18,000; on Cloudflare R2 egress (free) the egress is $0 and you pay only storage + Class B reads. The spread between cheapest and most expensive egress path is the entire game.

components.name: Video egress / CDN bandwidth
components.role: THE dominant cost (80-99% of opex at scale). Delivering TS segments to viewers. Cost = (GB/user/month) x (MAU) x ($/GB). Model 3.6 GB/user/mo at 1080p, ~30 eps. Per-provider $/GB: AWS CloudFront $0.085/GB (US/EU first 10TB, up to $0.14-0.17 APAC/India); bunny.net Standard $0.01/GB (NA/EU), $0.03 (Asia/Oceania), $0.045 (S.America), $0.06 (ME/Africa); bunny.net Volume $0.005/GB first 500TB, $0.004 to 1PB, $0.002 above 1PB; Cloudflare R2 egress = FREE (pay only $0.36/M Class B reads); Backblaze B2 egress FREE via Bandwidth Alliance/CDN partners (Cloudflare, bunny, Fastly), else free up to 3x stored then $0.01/GB; Hetzner EU traffic ~EUR1/TB overage (=$0.001/GB) with 20-60TB included per server; Contabo unmetered/fair-use; OVH unmetered on dedicated.
examples: 1M MAU x 3.6GB = 3.6PB/mo. CloudFront: ~$306,000/mo. bunny Volume: ~$18,000/mo. R2 egress: $0 (only ~$15k storage of full library + reads). Hetzner EU: ~$3,600/mo at EUR1/TB.
EMBED model: $0 — the upstream host (MegaCloud) serves the bytes; you serve only HTML+JS.
Proxy blow-up: running an m3u8/TS proxy on a $5 VPS at 1M MAU is impossible — 3.6PB through one box melts it; you must move to a CDN and pay full egress.

components.name: Object storage (self-host library)
components.role: Storing encoded HLS renditions. Anime library is large: ~120-380 MB/episode x multiple renditions (1080/720/480) ~= 0.5-0.8 GB/episode all-in. A 'good' catalog = 20k-50k episodes = ~15-40 TB. Only matters if self-hosting.
examples: Cloudflare R2: $0.015/GB-mo => 20TB = ~$300/mo, 40TB = ~$600/mo. Egress FREE. Class A writes $4.50/M, Class B reads $0.36/M.
Backblaze B2: $0.006/GB-mo ($6/TB) => 20TB = ~$120/mo; egress free via Cloudflare/bunny.
bunny Storage/Stream: from $0.01/GB-mo storage; Stream encoding is FREE (vs typical $0.02/min elsewhere).

components.name: Video encoding / transcode compute
components.role: FFmpeg transcode source -> HLS ABR ladder (H.264/H.265, 1080/720/480). One-time per title (CPU-heavy, ~1-3x realtime per rendition). Only for self-host. Largely avoidable.
examples: Self FFmpeg on Hetzner CCX/AX dedicated: a few hundred $/mo of compute clears a backlog, near $0 steady-state.
bunny.net Stream: transcoding FREE — pay only storage + delivery; eliminates encode compute entirely.
AWS MediaConvert ~$0.0075-0.015/min => encoding a 20k-episode/24-min library once = ~$3,600-7,200 one-time. Avoid by using FFmpeg or bunny.

components.name: Scraper + m3u8 extractor compute
components.role: Runs aniwatch/consumet-style scraper: fetch source pages, run MegaCloud/RapidCloud extractor (decrypt keys, parse JS, build m3u8). Lightweight: a few req/s of HTML + small decrypt. Cheap regardless of MAU because users hit your cache, not the source each time.
examples: Hetzner CPX21 (3 vCPU/4GB) EUR~6-8/mo or Contabo VPS $4.95-7/mo handles MVP-100k.
At 1M+ MAU: 2-4 nodes behind a queue + Redis cache of extracted links (links expire, so re-extract every few min) ~$50-150/mo.
Vercel/Render free-tier hosting of aniwatch-api works for dev only; you'll hit function timeouts + bandwidth caps fast.

components.name: m3u8/TS reverse proxy (the bandwidth trap)
components.role: Bypasses CORS + Referer/User-Agent locks on MegaCloud segments so the browser can play them on your domain. Rewrites playlist URIs to route segments through you. CRITICAL: if you proxy segments, you pay full video egress (see bandwidth row). If you only iframe-embed, you don't. This single design choice swings opex by 100x.
examples: MetaHat/m3u8-streaming-proxy, mediaflow-proxy (PyPI): inject headers, rewrite m3u8, in-memory cache.
Caching TS at the proxy de-dupes popular episodes but cache hit-rate on a long-tail anime catalog is modest; you still egress most bytes.
Cheap on a VPS at low scale; at 100k+ MAU it forces you onto a CDN and you eat full $/GB — negating the 'free' of embedding.

components.name: Residential / datacenter proxies for scraping
components.role: Source sites + Cloudflare in front of them block datacenter IPs; you rotate proxies to scrape. GB consumed is tiny (HTML + small JS, not video) so cost is low even though per-GB price is high.
examples: Webshare residential $1-2/GB (budget), datacenter from $0.50/IP; IPRoyal from ~$1.75/GB; Decodo/Smartproxy ~$3-8.5/GB ($75 min); Bright Data $4.20-12.82/GB ($499 for 39GB plan).
Scraping needs maybe 1-20 GB/mo of proxy traffic => $2-100/mo. Use cheap datacenter proxies + FlareSolverr first; escalate to residential only when blocked.
FlareSolverr (free, self-hosted headless Chrome) solves Cloudflare JS challenges — run it on the scraper VPS at ~$0 marginal.

components.name: Cloudflare (CDN/WAF/anti-bot in front of YOUR site)
components.role: Protects your origin, caches HTML/JS, absorbs DDoS, hides origin IP from DMCA. TOS CAVEAT: serving video through Cloudflare free CDN is restricted UNLESS the video is hosted on a Cloudflare service (R2/Stream/Images) — so R2-origin video CAN ride the free CDN legitimately; externally-hosted/proxied video cannot and risks account termination.
examples: Free plan: $0 (CDN, DDoS, SSL; no WAF/rate-limit/bot mgmt).
Pro $20/mo (annual) / $25 monthly: managed WAF, basic rate limiting. Business $200/mo (annual) / $250 monthly: custom WAF rules, custom SSL. Enterprise ~$3,000+/mo.
Bot Management add-on ~$30+/mo or Enterprise-only at full scale.

components.name: Domains + rotation (DMCA/takedown churn)
components.role: Piracy domains get seized/blocked (zoro.to -> aniwatch.to -> hianime.to evidences forced rotation). Budget for multiple TLDs + frequent moves. A legit/legal aggregator needs only one stable domain.
examples: ~$10-15/yr per .com; offshore/abuse-tolerant registrars (Njalla ~EUR15/yr, privacy-forward) for piracy.
Rotation overhead: 2-6 domains/yr + DNS migration time => ~$50-300/yr piracy; ~$15/yr legit.

components.name: Database + cache + metadata
components.role: Stores catalog, episode index, user lists, watch progress; Redis caches extracted m3u8 links (they expire). Modest cost; scales with users not video.
examples: Postgres on Hetzner VPS (self-managed) ~$0-10/mo; managed (Supabase/Neon free->$25/mo, RDS ~$15-50/mo small).
Redis: free on same VPS or Upstash free->$10/mo.

AniList GraphQL API: FREE under $150/mo revenue; ABOVE $150/mo revenue you need a COMMERCIAL LICENSE (contact AniList; price negotiated, not public). Bulk/backup hoarding of AniList data is prohibited — cache lightly or self-host metadata.

components.name: Dev time / labor (the real hidden cost)
components.role: Building + maintaining the scraper is a treadmill: MegaCloud changes encryption/keys frequently, breaking extractors; you patch constantly. This is why consumet/aniwatch repos churn and why hianime-style sites need ongoing engineering.
examples: Solo dev MVP: 2-6 weeks. Ongoing extractor maintenance: several hrs/week, indefinitely.
At freelance ~$50-100/hr, maintenance alone is ~$1,000-4,000/mo of opportunity cost even if you self-perform.
Legal aggregator (Crunchyroll/AnimeXin embeds, official APIs) drastically cuts this — no extractor arms race.

components.name: DMCA / legal exposure
components.role: Not a line-item you can buy down — it's existential for piracy. Hosts (R2/B2/bunny) will terminate on DMCA; registrars seize domains; payment processors and ad networks drop you. hianime shut down ~March-June 2026 and several repos were DMCA-blocked (aniwatch-api returns HTTP 451). [Unconfirmed exact shutdown date.]
examples: Mainstream ad networks (AdSense) ban piracy; you're forced into low-CPM popunder networks.
Legit path cost: content licensing is the wall — anime sublicensing is $$$$ and gated, which is why nobody bootstraps a legal Crunchyroll competitor cheaply.

howItWorks: TIER MODEL ASSUMPTIONS: 3.6 GB egress/user/month (30 eps x ~120 MB, 1080p HLS). MAU treated as ~monthly active streamers. Two columns per tier: EMBED (third-party serves video) vs SELF-HOST/PROXY (you serve video). Prices are 2025-2026 list rates.

MVP/DEV (~0-1k users): EMBED ~ $0-15/mo (Vercel/Render free + Contabo $5 scraper + $12/yr domain + Cloudflare Free + AniList free). SELF-HOST identical at this scale because traffic fits free tiers. Bandwidth: ~3.6 TB at 1k users — still trivial. TOTAL ~$5-20/mo. $/user ~$0.005-0.02.

10k MAU (36 TB/mo egress): EMBED ~ $20-60/mo (1-2 Contabo/Hetzner VPS for scraper+proxy of LINKS only, Cloudflare Free, domain, AniList free if revenue <$150). SELF-HOST/PROXY: 36 TB egress => bunny Volume ~$180/mo, bunny Standard(EU) ~$360/mo, R2 ~$0 egress + ~$300 storage(20TB) = ~$300/mo, CloudFront ~$3,060/mo (don't). TOTAL embed ~$30-60; self-host ~$200-400 (R2/bunny). $/user: embed ~$0.003-0.006; self-host ~$0.02-0.04.

100k MAU (360 TB/mo egress): EMBED ~ $100-300/mo (2-4 scraper nodes, Redis, Cloudflare Pro $20, residential proxy ~$50, DB ~$25; you likely cross AniList's $150 revenue line => need commercial license). SELF-HOST/PROXY: 360 TB => bunny Volume ~$1,800/mo, R2 egress $0 + storage(30TB ~$450) + Class B reads (~$50) = ~$500/mo, CloudFront ~$30,600/mo (lethal). Add ~$200-400 compute. TOTAL embed ~$150-350; self-host ~$1,000-2,500. $/user: embed ~$0.002-0.0035; self-host ~$0.01-0.025.

1M MAU (3.6 PB/mo egress): EMBED ~ $500-1,500/mo (CDN for HTML/JS only, 4-8 scraper/proxy-of-links nodes ~$300-800, Cloudflare Business $200, residential proxies ~$100-300, DB/cache ~$100, AniList commercial license $$$). SELF-HOST/PROXY: 3.6 PB => bunny Volume ~$18,000/mo (drops toward $0.004 over 500TB so ~$15-18k), R2 egress $0 + storage(40TB ~$600) + reads(~$500) = ~$1,100/mo + serving compute, Hetzner-fleet EU ~$3,600/mo at EUR1/TB, CloudFront ~$306,000/mo (bankrupt). TOTAL embed ~$1-3k; self-host ~$2-20k depending on path. $/user: embed ~$0.001-0.003; self-host (R2) ~$0.002-0.005, (bunny) ~$0.018, (CloudFront) ~$0.30.

10M MAU (36 PB/mo egress): EMBED still cheap relative to revenue ~ $3,000-15,000/mo (scaled scraper fleet, multiple proxy regions if proxying links, Cloudflare Business/Enterprise, heavy proxy IP spend, big Redis, AniList enterprise). SELF-HOST: 36 PB => bunny Volume ~$144,000/mo (blended ~$0.004), R2 egress $0 + storage + reads(~$5k reads) ~ $6-15k/mo + serving capacity, dedicated Hetzner/OVH fleet ~$36,000/mo at EUR1/TB plus box rental, CloudFront ~$3,060,000/mo (absurd). $/user: embed ~$0.0003-0.0015; self-host (R2) ~$0.001-0.0015, (bunny) ~$0.014.

THE COST THAT KILLS YOU = video egress on a metered CDN. The entire reason real piracy sites embed/iframe is to push that 3.6 PB onto the upstream host (MegaCloud/Filemoon/etc.) so their own egress stays near zero. The m3u8/TS proxy is the dangerous middle ground: it gives you a clean same-origin player and CORS bypass, but it pulls every video byte back through your infra, converting a $0 problem into a six-figure one at 1M+ MAU.

MINIMIZING EGRESS (how survivors do it): (1) iframe-embed the source player => $0 bandwidth (highest legal/UX risk, popups). (2) proxy only the .m3u8 manifest, let the BROWSER fetch .ts directly from the host => tiny proxy bandwidth, host pays segments (common compromise). (3) if you must proxy segments, use egress-free origins: Cloudflare R2 or Backblaze B2 through the Cloudflare Bandwidth Alliance, or bunny Volume at $0.005/GB. (4) cache hot episodes hard at the CDN edge. (5) NEVER serve video on AWS CloudFront/GCP/Azure at scale — metered egress is the bankruptcy path.

MONETIZATION REALITY (why $/user matters): AdSense bans piracy, so revenue comes from popunder/native/in-page networks (Adsterra, HilltopAds, ExoClick, PopAds, Clickadu). Anime piracy traffic skews Tier-2/3 (India, SEA, LATAM, Africa) where popunder CPMs are roughly $0.30-$2 (Tier-1 popunder/native ~$3-8, some pop ad case studies ~$5.35 avg CPM). Realistic blended ~$0.50-1.50 CPM. At ~30 page/video views per user/month, that's ~$0.015-0.045 ad revenue/user/month — which COMFORTABLY covers EMBED costs ($0.001-0.006/user) but is THIN or NEGATIVE against SELF-HOST on bunny ($0.018/user) and catastrophic on CloudFront. This math is exactly why the embed model dominates and self-hosting only survives on R2/B2 egress-free.

LEGIT/LEGAL ALTERNATIVE COSTS: A legal aggregator that embeds OFFICIAL players (Crunchyroll, YouTube official channels, Bilibili, official AnimeXin/Muse Asia embeds) has near-zero bandwidth (host serves it), no extractor arms race, can use AniList (commercial license) for metadata, and monetizes with mainstream ads/affiliate — infra ~$20-200/mo even at 100k+ MAU. The hard wall is CONTENT LICENSING (sublicensing anime is gated and very expensive), not infra. Self-hosting your OWN/public-domain or licensed content on R2+Cloudflare is the only path where self-hosting bandwidth is actually affordable.

keyRepos.name: ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch (npm: aniwatch)
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Node/TS scraper package for hianime.to: catalog/search/episode data plus the MegaCloud (and vidstreaming) extractor that decrypts and returns the m3u8 URL, Referer/User-Agent headers, and .vtt subtitles. Surviving npm artifact even where the API repo is DMCA-blocked. Quality keyword (1080/720) selects ABR variant.

keyRepos.name: ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch-api
keyRepos.whatItProvides: REST wrapper around the aniwatch scraper, Docker-deployable on :4000, returns m3u8 + headers + subs. NOTE: the GitHub repo now returns HTTP 451 (Unavailable For Legal Reasons / DMCA-blocked) — rely on forks, npm, Docker images, web archive. It returns SOURCE links; it does not itself proxy video bytes.

keyRepos.name: consumet/consumet.ts + api.consumet.org
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Multi-provider media metadata + stream-link library (anime/movies/manga). Public hosted API is DOWN — self-host required (docker pull riimuru/consumet-api, run on :3000; deploy to Vercel/Render/Railway). Repo still updated as of Mar 2026. Returns source stream links, not proxied bytes.

keyRepos.name: MetaHat/m3u8-streaming-proxy
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Node HLS proxy purpose-built for hianime: rewrites .m3u8, proxies .ts segments, CORS bypass + header injection (Referer/User-Agent), in-memory cache, origin-lock, rate-limit, Docker; one-click Render/Vercel deploy. THIS is the component that triggers the bandwidth blow-up if you route segments through it.

keyRepos.name: mediaflow-proxy (PyPI)
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Python HLS/MPD proxy: injects headers, rewrites playlists so segment requests route through the proxy; alternative to the Node m3u8 proxy. Same bandwidth-cost implication — all proxied bytes are your egress.

keyRepos.name: Backblaze B2 + Cloudflare Bandwidth Alliance
keyRepos.whatItProvides: $0.006/GB-mo storage with FREE egress when routed through Cloudflare/bunny/Fastly (else free up to 3x stored then $0.01/GB). The canonical egress-free self-host stack alongside R2.

technologies: FFmpeg (HLS ABR transcode: H.264/H.265, 1080/720/480 ladder)
HLS / m3u8 / .ts segments (10s chunks; ~6.5 Mbps 1080p => ~120 MB / 24-min episode)
Cloudflare R2 (egress-free object storage, $0.015/GB-mo, Class B reads $0.36/M)
Backblaze B2 ($0.006/GB-mo, egress-free via Bandwidth Alliance/CDN partners)
bunny.net CDN ($0.01/GB EU/NA standard; Volume $0.005->$0.002/GB) + bunny Stream (FREE encoding)
AWS CloudFront ($0.085/GB US/EU first 10TB) / MediaConvert (~$0.0075-0.015/min) — the expensive path to avoid for video
Hetzner Cloud/Dedicated (CPX VPS EUR6-8, EU traffic 20-60TB incl, EUR1/TB overage)
Contabo VPS ($4.95-7/mo, unmetered fair-use traffic) / OVH (unmetered dedicated)
Cloudflare (Free / Pro $20 / Business $200 / Enterprise ~$3k+; WAF, rate-limit, Bot Management add-on ~$30+)
FlareSolverr (self-hosted headless-Chrome Cloudflare-challenge solver, free)
Residential/datacenter proxies (Webshare $1-2/GB, IPRoyal $1.75/GB, Decodo/Smartproxy $3-8.5/GB, Bright Data $4.20-12.82/GB)
Redis/Upstash (cache short-lived extracted m3u8 links)
PostgreSQL / Supabase / Neon (catalog + user lists)
AniList GraphQL API (metadata; free <$150/mo revenue, commercial license required above)
Adsterra / HilltopAds / ExoClick / PopAds / Clickadu (popunder/native ad monetization, ~$0.30-2 CPM Tier-2/3)
Docker (deploy scraper, extractor, proxy)
gotchas: BANDWIDTH IS THE ONLY NUMBER THAT MATTERS at scale. A $5 scraper VPS is irrelevant; 3.6 PB/mo of video egress is the whole budget. Model $/GB FIRST, everything else second.

The m3u8/TS PROXY BLOW-UP: routing segments through your proxy converts a $0 (embed) problem into full CDN egress. At 1M MAU that's the difference between ~$1k/mo and ~$18k (bunny) or ~$306k (CloudFront). Proxy ONLY the manifest if you can; let browsers fetch .ts from the upstream host.
NEVER serve video at scale on AWS CloudFront / GCP / Azure — metered egress ($0.085-0.17/GB) is mathematically a bankruptcy machine for free video. This is the single most common fatal mistake.

Cloudflare Free/Pro/Business CDN PROHIBITS serving video/large non-HTML files UNLESS hosted on a Cloudflare service (R2/Stream/Images). R2-origin video can legitimately ride the free CDN; proxied/externally-hosted video on the free CDN risks account termination (old Section 2.8, replaced 2023 but the video restriction for externally-hosted content remains).
Backblaze B2 'free egress' has conditions: must route through a partner CDN (Cloudflare/bunny/Fastly); direct API GETs beyond 3x stored cost $0.01/GB. Don't assume unconditional free.
Contabo/Hetzner 'unlimited/unmetered' is fair-use — saturating 10Gbps 24/7 with PB-scale video will get you throttled or warned; it is NOT a free CDN substitute at high MAU.
Extractor maintenance is a TREADMILL: MegaCloud/RapidCloud rotate encryption keys and obfuscation, breaking aniwatch/consumet extractors repeatedly. Budget continuous dev time, not a one-time build.
AniList: crossing $150/mo revenue requires a commercial license (price negotiated privately); hoarding/backup of their data is banned. Don't build your catalog as an AniList mirror.

Monetization ceiling: piracy is locked out of AdSense; popunder/native CPMs on Tier-2/3 anime traffic are low ($0.30-2). Revenue ~$0.015-0.045/user/mo only covers EMBED economics — it does NOT cover self-hosting on metered CDNs.

DMCA churn is structural: domains get seized (zoro->aniwatch->hianime), repos get 451'd, hosts terminate. hianime reportedly shut down ~March-June 2026. The legal/legit path trades infra cost for a CONTENT-LICENSING wall that is far more expensive than any server bill.
Cache hit-rate on a long-tail anime catalog is modest — don't assume edge caching erases your egress; most bytes still leave the origin.

refuted.claim: hianime shutdown window 'March-June 2026' is per task brief and not independently confirmed; treat date as approximate/unconfirmed.
refuted.note: This is now INDEPENDENTLY CONFIRMED and far more precise than 'approximate.' HiAnime first went offline 13 March 2026 with a farewell message; the Discord team officially declared permanent closure on 31 May 2026 after 80 days of inactivity. The shutdown was tied to a 13 March 2026 ACE court win ($18.75M judgment) and USTR 'notorious markets' listing. The 'March-June 2026' window is accurate but the brief's hedge that it is 'unconfirmed' is itself outdated — multiple outlets (CBR, Beebom, OtakuKart) confirm it.

refuted.claim: Per-episode size ~120 MB at 1080p HLS (~6.5 Mbps); 3.6 GB/user/mo assumes ~30 eps; engagement varies 2-10x scaling cost linearly.
refuted.note: CORE MATH ERROR. The two anchors are mutually inconsistent: 6.5 Mbps x 24 min = 6.5 x 1440 / 8 = ~1,170 MB (~1.14 GB), NOT 120 MB. A 24-min episode would only be 120 MB at ~0.67 Mbps average — that is 360-480p, not 1080p. Industry data: 1080p streaming ~= 50 MB/min ~= 3 GB/hr, so a 24-min 1080p episode is realistically ~1.0-1.4 GB at 5-8 Mbps, or ~720 MB even at a lean 4 Mbps. Even official 1080p anime DOWNLOADS are ~230-300 MB (brief said ~380 MB — overstated) thanks to slow-paced offline encodes; live ABR streaming is HIGHER, not lower, than the 120 MB claim implies. The 'ABR rarely exceeds 200 MB/episode' line is also wrong for genuine 1080p. The engagement-varies-2-10x and linear-scaling logic is sound, but it scales a wrong base number. If the true 1080p anchor is ~1 GB/ep, every downstream cost (3.6 GB/user/mo -> ~30 GB; 3.6 PB/mo -> ~30 PB) and every dollar figure is understated by roughly 8x. HEVC at low CRF could pull a 1080p episode toward ~300-500 MB, but not 120 MB.

confirmed: AniList commercial license PRICE is not public; $150/mo revenue threshold confirmed; actual license fee negotiated via email — dollar figure unconfirmed.
Popunder/native CPMs ($0.30-2 Tier-2/3, $3-8 Tier-1) are network/bid dependent and swing widely; case studies ~$5.35 avg, outliers $0.2-$25; treat as ranges.
Provider list prices change: Hetzner raised cloud prices ~Apr 2026 (+~33% some plans) and adjusted again 15 Jun 2026; bunny/R2/CloudFront rates are 2025-2026 snapshots.
bunny.net Volume Network has only ~10 PoPs (lower performance than Standard's 119); $0.005/GB trades latency for price; real-world QoS may push you to Standard at 2-12x cost.
aniwatch-api repo HTTP 451 (DMCA-blocked) observed; forks/npm/Docker mirrors are surviving artifacts and may also disappear.
corrections: CRITICAL (Claim 4): The 120 MB/episode anchor is wrong and internally contradicts the stated 6.5 Mbps. 6.5 Mbps x 24 min / 8 = ~1,170 MB (~1.14 GB), not 120 MB. A 24-min episode is only 120 MB at ~0.67 Mbps (360-480p quality). Real 1080p streaming = ~50 MB/min = ~3 GB/hr, so a genuine 1080p anime episode is ~1.0-1.4 GB streamed (or ~720 MB at a lean 4 Mbps). Replace the anchor with ~1 GB/episode at true 1080p (or explicitly state the 120 MB figure assumes ~480-540p / aggressive HEVC, NOT 1080p).

DOWNSTREAM (Claim 4): Because all of (3.6 GB/user/mo, 3.6 PB/mo at 1M MAU, $306K CloudFront, $18K bunny) are derived from the 120 MB anchor, they are understated by roughly 8x at true 1080p. Corrected order-of-magnitude: ~30 GB/user/mo, ~30 PB/mo, ~$2.4M/mo undiscounted CloudFront (less after volume tiers), ~$144K/mo bunny Volume (~$60K at $0.002 PB-rate). R2 egress stays $0. The 100x architecture-spread thesis is UNCHANGED and still correct.

Claim 4 sub-error: 'official 1080p downloads ~380 MB' is overstated. Typical 1080p anime torrent encodes are ~230-300 MB for a 24-min episode (anime compresses well due to still scenes). Use ~250-300 MB.

Claim 4 sub-error: 'ABR streaming rarely exceeds 200 MB/episode' is false for genuine 1080p — live ABR streaming generally EXCEEDS offline download size at equal resolution, and 1080p is ~1 GB. 200 MB/episode corresponds to ~480p.
Claim 2: Update the hedge — the HiAnime shutdown is no longer 'unconfirmed/approximate.' It is independently confirmed: offline 13 March 2026, permanent closure declared 31 May 2026.

Claim 7: Soften the 'account review' warning — it is not documented in Cloudflare's ToS/blog; serving R2-hosted video via CDN at scale is explicitly permitted under the updated ToS. Present the review risk as speculative, not as a known policy.

Claim 6: '~10 PoPs' for bunny Volume is from secondary sources, not an official published count; phrase as 'a small subset of PoPs (reportedly ~10)'. The 2-12x cost multiplier vs Standard is accurate (Standard Asia-Pacific $0.06/GB vs Volume $0.005/GB = 12x; NA/EU $0.01 vs $0.005 = 2x).
additionalFindings: bunny Volume drops to $0.002/GB at petabyte scale (not just the $0.005 entry rate), so at PB-scale streaming the bunny figure is even lower than the brief's $18K — relevant to the cost-spread argument.

CloudFront at PB scale tiers DOWN to ~$0.02/GB (US/EU) per current 2026 pricing, so the $306K figure ($0.085 flat) is the worst-case undiscounted number; a PB-scale operator would pay materially less (~$72K-$200K depending on tier blending). Still bankrupting vs R2/bunny, so the qualitative point holds.

Backblaze B2 + Cloudflare Bandwidth Alliance is STILL ACTIVE in 2026 (free egress via Cloudflare/Fastly/bunny CDN paths). This is a second egress-free path the brief mentions; confirmed live. Caveat: free only for CDN/download routing through Cloudflare, NOT compute-to-storage GETs (those pay $0.01/GB).

R2 current 2026 pricing confirmed: Standard storage $0.015/GB-mo, Class A $4.50/M, Class B $0.36/M; egress free via S3 API, Workers, and r2.dev. Free tier 10 GB + 1M Class A + 10M Class B. At PB-scale self-host, the real R2 cost is storage (catalog size, roughly fixed) + Class B reads (segment GETs) — HLS chunking multiplies Class B ops, so segment count/duration becomes a non-trivial line item the brief underweights.
Consumet public hosted API is no longer available — self-hosting is now required (reinforces brief's point that the aggregator tooling is fragile/disappearing).

The embed-vs-proxy 'proxy blow-up' thesis is corroborated: the ZoroTheme family (HiAnime/AniWatch/Kaido/9AnimeTV) relies on MegaCloud/RapidCloud/VidCloud as the actual video hosts, and one backend powers dozens of front-ends — confirming the third-party-pays-bandwidth architecture (A).

Cloudflare explicitly documents (Delivering Videos with Cloudflare) that video hosted OUTSIDE Cloudflare is restricted on the CDN — so the embed/proxy architecture cannot legally lean on Cloudflare's free CDN for externally-hosted MegaCloud streams; you must host on R2/Stream to get the egress-free path. This is a sharper constraint than the brief states.

layer: Monetization — revenue models and real economics for free anime streaming sites (piracy-grade vs legit/clean)
overview: Free anime streaming sites are pure ad-arbitrage businesses: traffic is acquired for ~free (SEO + brand recall on a leeched library), infrastructure is pushed onto third-party video hosts (StreamTape/MegaCloud/DoodStream class) so the operator's marginal cost per stream is near zero, and the operator monetizes raw eyeballs through the lowest-friction, no-questions-asked ad networks. Because mainstream demand (Google AdSense/AdX, Amazon, most SSPs) explicitly bans copyright-infringing sites, the entire piracy ad stack is "grey-market" pop/push/direct-link networks (PopAds, Adsterra, PropellerAds, HilltopAds, Clickadu, PopCash, Galaksion, Adcash, ExoClick/Hilltop adult-adjacent) plus CPA/affiliate (VPNs, dating/adult smartlinks, gaming, manga/figure stores) and a thin "premium no-ads / donation" layer. The economics are volume-driven and low-quality: piracy-grade effective RPM (revenue per 1,000 pageviews) lands roughly $0.50–$3 blended (heavily geo-dependent; ~40% of HiAnime traffic was US tier-1, ~25% India tier-3), versus a CLEAN/legit anime site (licensed catalog, reviews, news, simulcast aggregator) that can run AdSense/Ezoic/Mediavine at entertainment-niche RPMs of $5–$15 and add legit affiliate (Crunchyroll, VPN, merch via Amazon Associates) plus a real subscription tier. HiAnime at its 2024 peak (~364M monthly visits, global rank ~#120) was credibly estimated to gross on the order of $400k/month from ads alone; it shut down ~March–June 2026 after ACE/USTR pressure. The model that survives legal scrutiny is the clean-site one: display + legit affiliate + freemium subscription; the piracy stack collapses the moment payment processors (Visa/MC/PayPal) and DMCA-integrated ad networks de-platform you.

components.name: Popunder / on-click networks (PopAds, PopCash, Adsterra, PropellerAds, HilltopAds, Clickadu, Galaksion)
components.role: The #1 revenue source for piracy-grade anime sites. A click anywhere on the player/page spawns a full-page ad in a new tab/behind the window. No content review, instant approval, daily/biweekly payouts, anti-adblock baked in. Sells inventory by geo on RTB so US/UK pay multiples of India/SEA.
examples: PopAds: US/UK popunder up to ~$4–$6 CPM, mobile Android ~$4.50, desktop ~$3.50; tier-2/3 ~$2–$4; floor ~$0.50. Min payout $5, PAID DAILY (PayPal/BTC/wire $500). Claims ~2B daily views, 98.5% 'clean' traffic. Self-reported earnings: ~$847/mo on a 50k-visit tech blog

Adsterra popunder: US ~$2–$8 CPM, English tech blog hit ~$3.20 popunder CPM in Q4 2024; India ~$0.10–$0.50. Min payout $5 (Paxum) / $100 (BTC/PayPal/USDT) / $1000 (wire), paid twice monthly (1st & 16th), 2-week hold. Formats: popunder, Social Bar, Direct Link, native, push
PropellerAds: ~12B daily impressions, 32k+ advertisers; popunder + push CPS model. Clickadu: ~5B daily impressions. Galaksion / PopCash / Adcash: PopCash min payout $10, paid via PayPal/Payoneer/BTC
Practical blended popunder eCPM for an anime site (mixed geo, ~40% US): ~$1.0–$2.5; fill rate effectively ~100% (house/remnant fallback)

components.name: Push notification subscriptions (PropellerAds CPS, RichPush, Adsterra, Megapush)
components.role: Visitor is prompted to 'allow notifications'; the network then pays per subscriber and/or per push impression, monetizing the user for weeks AFTER they leave. Recurring, ad-block-resistant. Heavily used by piracy sites because it converts a one-time visit into a lifetime annuity.
examples: Push CPM: tier-1 ~$1–$2+, tier-2/3 ~$0.10–$0.50; PropellerAds floor ~$0.01
Worked example (industry): 300k subscribers at $0.10 CPM, 1 push/day = ~$30/day = ~$900/mo of 'lifetime' revenue from already-captured users
CPS (cost-per-subscription) model: paid a flat fee per opted-in push subscriber, geo-dependent (cents to ~$0.30+ tier-1)

components.name: Direct link / interstitial / redirect & Social Bar
components.role: High-CPM full-page or redirect placements triggered on play/episode-change, plus 'social bar' (fake notification widget). Maximizes revenue per session on piracy sites at the cost of UX. Banned on any AdSense-monetized clean site.
examples: Adsterra Social Bar: mobile CTR ~2x traditional banners; Direct Link/Smartlink monetizes clicks/redirects with no ad slot
Interstitial/redirect CPMs track popunder rates (tier-1 ~$2–$8); used as the 'first click = ad' layer

components.name: In-player video pre-roll / VAST & SSAI
components.role: Video ads injected before/around the episode via a VAST tag. Highest CPM format BUT premium video demand (the $12–$50 CPM inventory) refuses piracy; piracy sites get only grey-market VAST fill from ExoClick/HilltopAds/Adsterra video at far lower rates. SSAI (server-side ad insertion) stitches ads into the stream to beat ad-blockers, but piracy operators rarely run real SSAI because they don't own the origin (3rd-party hosts do).
examples: Legit/premium pre-roll: non-skippable ~$14 CPM, skippable ~$12; CTV $20–$60 — these advertisers will NOT appear on piracy sites
Grey-market in-player VAST on piracy: realistically ~$1–$5 CPM, low fill, frequent blank/passback
SSAI value prop: ad-block-proof impressions, but requires controlling the manifest/origin

components.name: Affiliate & CPA networks (VPN, dating/adult, gaming, manga/figures)
components.role: Banner/interstitial 'Watch with VPN' or anime-adjacent offers paid per signup/sale (CPA) or revenue share. Often the HIGHEST-margin layer for anime sites because the audience genuinely wants VPNs and merch, and CPA networks don't police publisher legality.
examples: VPN CPA: NordVPN up to 100% of a 1-month plan + 30% recurring on renewals (40% on 1/2-yr); ExpressVPN fixed ~$13 (1mo)/$22 (6mo)/$36 (12mo) per paid signup, $100 min payout
CrakRevenue (adult/dating CPA + Smartlink): Victoria Milan $75 PPS, Ashley Madison up to $70 PPS, AdultFriendFinder $60 PPS; 250+ geos, 300+ offers auto-optimized; >$10M paid via Smartlinks
Legit affiliate for a CLEAN site: Crunchyroll affiliate, Amazon Associates on figures/manga (~3–4% category rate), Right Stuf/merch — lower payout but advertiser-safe
Gaming/app-install CPA and crypto/sweepstakes smartlinks fill the rest

components.name: Premium 'no-ads' subscription tier
components.role: Freemium upsell: pay ~$2–$5/mo (positioned as 'donation for servers', far under Crunchyroll/Netflix) to remove ads / unlock 1080p. Survivable on a legit site; on piracy sites it's the layer that gets you killed because it requires a card processor that will drop you for infringement.
examples: Conversion benchmarks: freemium-to-paid industry median ~2–5% (good 3–5%, excellent 6–8%); ad-supported→paid streaming often 2–3% (Spotify is an outlier at ~40%+ in some markets)
Worked: 1M MAU clean site, 3% convert at $4/mo = ~$120k/mo gross (minus processor fees/churn)
Piracy reality: tiny conversion + processor bans = usually framed as 'donation' instead

components.name: Donations / Patreon / crypto tips
components.role: Voluntary support framed as keeping the site alive. Small absolute dollars; Patreon/processor risk for infringing or adult-adjacent content. Crypto (BTC/XMR) used to dodge processor bans.
examples: Patreon/SubscribeStar exposure: Visa/Mastercard pressure has de-platformed creators (esp. adult/'brand-damaging'); 2021 Mastercard adult-merchant standards tightened this — same lever hits piracy/donation pages
Crypto donation addresses (BTC/Monero) are the de-facto fallback when card rails refuse the operator

components.name: Crypto mining / cryptojacking (historical)
components.role: 2017–2019 era: embed Coinhive-class JS to mine Monero in visitors' browsers, monetizing CPU instead of ad views. Largely dead post-2019.
examples: Coinhive: ~62% of all in-browser miners, ~$250k/mo at peak; The Pirate Bay was the first big adopter; shut down 8 Mar 2019 after XMR fell ~85% — model now economically dead and reputation-toxic

components.name: Data monetization (cookies/trackers)
components.role: Selling/sharing visitor data and behavioral segments to data brokers/ad partners. Quietly present on piracy sites; legally radioactive under GDPR/CCPA for a clean operator.
examples: Cited as a secondary revenue stream for anime piracy sites via cookies/trackers sold for targeted advertising — unquantified, opaque

components.name: Clean/legit display stack (AdSense, Ezoic, Mediavine/Raptive)
components.role: The ONLY mainstream high-quality demand — available exclusively to advertiser-safe sites (licensed/aggregator/news/reviews, NOT infringing streams). This is the monetization path a developer building legit can actually use.
examples: AdSense entertainment-niche RPM ~$3–$10; Ezoic similar-to-better with header bidding; Mediavine ~$20–$40 RPM (requires 50k+ monthly sessions and original content)
Entertainment-niche RPM band overall ~$5–$15 — multiples of piracy-grade blended RPM despite lower raw traffic

howItWorks: Traffic → ad-tech waterfall: A pageview/episode-play fires (1) a popunder/on-click impression, (2) optional in-player VAST pre-roll, (3) banner/native slots, (4) a push-permission prompt, and (5) affiliate/CPA creatives. Each layer is a separate network billing on CPM, CPS, or CPA, stacked to maximize revenue per session.

Geo is destiny: RTB bids on grey-market networks vary ~10–50x by country. US/UK/CA/AU popunders fetch ~$2–$6 CPM; India/SEA/LatAm ~$0.10–$1. HiAnime's ~40% US / ~25% India mix means a minority of pageviews produce the majority of revenue. Blended piracy eCPM lands ~$0.50–$3.

Cost externalization: The operator does NOT pay for licensing (vs Crunchyroll/Netflix paying tens of millions) and does NOT pay bandwidth for the actual video — third-party hosts (MegaCloud/StreamTape/DoodStream class) store/serve files and historically paid uploaders ~$10–$25 per 10,000 views. The operator runs only a thin frontend + m3u8/extractor proxy, so margin on ad revenue is extremely high.

Why the grey stack at all: AdSense/AdX and nearly all premium SSPs explicitly prohibit sites that host or link to copyrighted anime; Google auto-pulls ads from any page hit by a valid DMCA notice and permanently blocks future AdSense on it. So piracy sites are structurally locked out of the $5–$15 RPM demand and confined to $0.50–$3 pop/push demand.

Recurring monetization beyond the visit: Push subscriptions and CPA renewals (e.g., NordVPN 30% recurring) turn a one-time visitor into ongoing revenue, partly offsetting the low per-view eCPM and ad-blocker leakage.

The legit alternative inverts the stack: a clean aggregator/news/reviews site (no infringing streams) qualifies for AdSense/Ezoic/Mediavine ($5–$40 RPM), legit affiliate (Crunchyroll, Amazon figures/manga, VPN), and a real card-processed subscription — far higher RPM per visitor, but it must EARN traffic (no leeched library to ride) and cannot host the streams that drive piracy traffic.

keyRepos.name: consumet.ts / consumet API
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Open-source scraping/aggregation library that powered many anime frontends — relevant to monetization because it lets a builder run a thin frontend with zero content cost, the structural precondition for the ad-arbitrage model (many such repos DMCA-pressured 2024–2026; verify current status).

keyRepos.name: aniwatch-api (ghoshRitesh12)
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Unofficial HiAnime/aniwatch scraper API + npm package; the kind of backend that makes the near-zero-marginal-cost piracy economics possible. Several such repos were DMCA-blocked around the 2026 shutdown — rely on npm artifacts/forks/mirrors; mark availability as unconfirmed.

technologies: Pop/on-click networks: PopAds, PopCash, Adsterra, PropellerAds, HilltopAds, Clickadu, Galaksion, Adcash, AdMaven
Push-notification monetization: PropellerAds (CPS), RichPush, Megapush, Adsterra push
In-player video/VAST grey-market demand: ExoClick, HilltopAds video, Adsterra video
CPA/affiliate: NordVPN/ExpressVPN/Surfshark affiliate, CrakRevenue (dating/adult Smartlink), gaming/app-install CPA
Clean-site demand: Google AdSense/AdX, Ezoic, Mediavine/Raptive, Amazon Associates, Crunchyroll affiliate
Subscription/payment rails: Stripe/PayPal (clean sites only); crypto (BTC/Monero) as processor-ban fallback
Historical: Coinhive (Monero browser mining, dead since Mar 2019)
gotchas: AdSense and virtually all premium demand REFUSE piracy: Google policy bans monetizing sites that host or link to copyrighted anime; a valid DMCA notice strips ads from the page and permanently blocks future AdSense. This is why piracy sites can never touch $5–$15 RPM demand — they're capped at $0.50–$3 grey-market eCPM.

Ad-blocker leakage is huge in this audience: anime/tech-savvy users run uBlock/Brave at high rates, so a large share of 'pageviews' never render an ad. Revenue estimates assuming ~50% see ads (e.g., HiAnime ~$400k/mo) are upper-bound, not booked revenue.
Per-view payout is brutally low: cited ~$0.004 per monetized anime-piracy view — the business only works at 100M+ monthly visits. At small scale piracy monetization is not worth the legal exposure.

Payment-processor bans kill subscriptions/donations: Visa/Mastercard/PayPal drop infringing (and adjacent NSFW) merchants; 2021 Mastercard adult-merchant rules and ongoing pressure de-platformed Patreon/SubscribeStar creators. A piracy 'premium tier' has no durable card rail — hence 'donations' and crypto.

Grey networks have hidden frictions: shaving (under-counted impressions), high min-payout tiers (Adsterra $100 BTC/$1000 wire), 2-week holds, and account terminations for 'invalid traffic'. Payouts are real but lossy and unstable.

Pop/push/redirect ads carry malware/scam creatives (fake-update, dating, sweepstakes) — this is what gets a site flagged by Google Safe Browsing and ad-blocker lists, further suppressing fill and reputation.
Crypto mining is dead economics: post-Coinhive (Mar 2019) and XMR's ~85% drop, browser mining yields cents and instant reputation/AV-flag damage — do not consider it.

Legit RPMs require EARNED traffic and original content: Mediavine needs 50k+ sessions and original, advertiser-safe content; an aggregator can't just reskin a leeched library and expect $20–$40 RPM. The clean model trades higher per-visitor revenue for much harder traffic acquisition.
Tier-mix dominates the P&L: a 'clean' site with 100k US pageviews can out-earn a piracy site with 1M tier-3 pageviews. Always model revenue by geo, not flat CPM.

confirmed: Claim 1 (MODELED) — Piracy-grade monthly ad+CPA revenue: ~100k PV = $50-$250/mo; ~1M = $500-$2,500/mo; ~10M = $5k-$25k/mo; blended RPM ~$0.50-$2.5.
Claim 2 (MODELED) — Clean/legit site: ~100k PV = $500-$1,500/mo (RPM $5-15); ~1M = $5k-$15k ads + ~$120k/mo sub IF 1M paid-eligible MAU at 3%/$4; ~10M = $50k-$150k ads. Clean RPM ~3-20x piracy RPM.
Claim 3 (UNCONFIRMED) — HiAnime ~$400k/month from ads at ~331-364M monthly visits is a media ESTIMATE (assumes ~50% see ads, unspecified eCPM), not disclosed financials.
Claim 4 (UNCONFIRMED) — 'PopAds was HiAnime's primary network' is reported by secondary sources (animexnews), consistent with niche, but actual networks HiAnime ran are not publicly verified.
Claim 5 — Network CPMs/payout terms (PopAds ~$4 US, Adsterra holds, VPN/CrakRevenue payouts) are from network review pages/operator marketing as of 2024-2026 and change frequently; re-check live.
Claim 6 (UNCONFIRMED) — Third-party video host payout '$10-$25 per 10,000 views' references older hosts (Openload/Streamango, now defunct); current MegaCloud-class host economics are opaque.
Claim 7 — HiAnime shutdown window (~March-June 2026) and USTR notorious-markets listing (early March 2026) per Wikipedia/CBR; treat exact dates as reported, not primary.
corrections: Claim 3 says the $400k estimate uses an 'unspecified eCPM' — this is wrong. The source (AnimeXNews) explicitly assumes ~$0.004 per monetized view, i.e. a ~$4 CPM (matching PopAds US tier-1). The eCPM IS specified; only the network mix is not. Note the source's own formula (331M x 50% x $0.004 = ~$662k) actually yields more than $400k, so $400k is a conservative restatement of their math.

Claim 4: No source states PopAds was HiAnime's PRIMARY network. AnimeXNews names PopAds only as a generic example for piracy sites as a class ('such as PopAds'), not as HiAnime's specific or primary vendor. The attribution should be softened from 'primary network (reported)' to 'PopAds cited as a category example; HiAnime's actual networks are unverified.'

Claim 2 subscription layer: equating 'pageviews' with 'paid-eligible MAU' is the load-bearing weakness. 1M monthly pageviews is NOT 1M monthly active users; the $120k/mo sub figure assumes a conversion BASE that pageviews overstate by a large factor. The claim half-hedges this ('IF 1M are paid-eligible MAU'), but the 'sub layer dwarfs ads' conclusion only holds under that unrealistic equivalence and should be downgraded to illustrative.

Claim 7: the '40% US tier-1 / 25% India tier-3' traffic split used elsewhere in the overview is only partly verified — TorrentFreak confirms 'roughly 40%' US, but I could NOT confirm a specific ~25% India figure (Similarweb/Semrush only confirm India as the #2 country; exact percentage is paywalled). Treat the India 25% as unverified.
additionalFindings: The US ~40% traffic share IS independently confirmed by TorrentFreak ('roughly 40% of the site's visits are from users in the United States'), and the SimilarWeb global rank ~#120 is confirmed for the Oct 2024 peak. The India share (claimed ~25%) is only confirmed as 'second-largest country,' not the specific percentage — flag as estimate.

Ad-network eligibility thresholds shifted materially in late 2025 / early 2026, which affects Claim 2's 'clean site' premise: Raptive (ex-AdThrive) dropped from 100k to 25k monthly pageviews (Oct 2025) but requires >=50% tier-1 traffic for sub-100k sites; Mediavine added a $5,000/yr-ad-revenue alternative path (effective Jan 15, 2026) and runs 'Journey' at 1,000+ sessions with a 70% rev share. So a 100k-PV clean anime site can plausibly access premium networks, strengthening the upper-end RPM assumption — but the access path is different from the simple 'just sign up for Mediavine' implied.

MegaCloud is best understood as a captive/embedded player within the zoro->aniwatch->HiAnime lineage (scraped by consumet / aniwatch-api), not an independent revenue-share file host like Openload/Streamango. That means the 'host pays uploader $10-25/10k views' model in Claim 6 likely does NOT describe MegaCloud at all — for a captive host the video-delivery margin accrues to the operator, not to a third-party uploader. This makes the Openload analogy structurally misleading for the specific case study, even though the cited number is accurately attributed.

Broader-context corroboration for Claim 7: the March 2026 crackdown was not isolated — CBR and OtakuKart report 9anime/AnimeKai going dark and Vietnam-hosted operations being targeted in the same window, plus a ~$18.75M ACE judgment and METI/anime-industry loss figures (~2.3 trillion yen / ~$15.1B cited by CBR; a separate METI survey cited ~5.7 trillion yen / ~$38B for all Japanese content). These macro loss numbers are themselves industry-advocacy estimates and should not be treated as hard financials.

Overall: the strongest single source underpinning the monetization narrative (the $400k estimate, PopAds, $10-25/10k-views host payout, 'grey-market ad stack') is ONE article, animexnews.com — a niche secondary outlet doing back-of-envelope math, not primary or audited data. The entire revenue thesis rests on a thin evidentiary base. Mainstream outlets (TorrentFreak, CBR) corroborate only the traffic and shutdown facts, never the revenue. The author's repeated 'MODELED/UNCONFIRMED/ESTIMATE' labels are appropriate and should be preserved verbatim.

layer: Pitfalls & Failure Modes (the complete map: technical, legal, operational, financial, security/reputation, business)
overview: Running a free anime piracy/aggregator site is less a product and more a continuous loss-mitigation exercise across six failure domains. TECHNICAL: the whole stack is a "breakage treadmill" — extractors like the consumet/aniwatch MegaCloud decryptor die every time the upstream rotates an AES key or a domain (e.g. megacloud.blog expiring -> megacloud.tv; the Eggwite "megacloud-key-extractor" is now marked NO LONGER FUNCTIONAL), Cloudflare Turnstile/bot-management kills HTTP-only scrapers, m3u8 proxies hit CORS/codec/bandwidth-runaway, and Widevine L1 HD DRM is effectively unbreakable for a hobbyist (so you're stuck scraping other pirates, inheriting their fragility). LEGAL: the existential category — the U.S. Protecting Lawful Streaming Act (2020) makes commercial streaming a felony (up to 3-5-10 years), and there are real convictions: Jetflicks operator Kristopher Dallmann sentenced to 7 years (2025); B9GOOD operator got 3 years (China, 2023, CODA case); Fmovies operators arrested in Vietnam (2024, ACE); GitHub nuked 900+ anime repos including aniwatch/aniwatch-api/MegacloudKeys (March 2026, Remove Your Media/Crunchyroll); HiAnime and 9anime shut down ~March 2026 after two years on the USTR Notorious Markets list; Cloudflare hit with ~$24M Japanese judgment (Nov 2025) for serving manga-piracy CDN. OPERATIONAL: every infrastructure dependency can be cut — CDN/host bans, Cloudflare repeat-infringer termination, AdSense permanently blocking DMCA'd pages, Stripe/PayPal banning the category outright. FINANCIAL: video egress can outrun ad revenue (CloudFront ~$0.085/GB vs Bunny ~$0.005/min vs free-egress R2), residential-proxy scraping costs $3-8.40/GB, and chargebacks above 1% trigger processor termination. SECURITY/REPUTATION: legit ad networks won't touch you, so you take popunder/malvertising networks (malvertising = ~12% of pirate-site ads, ~$121M/yr; ~8 in 10 pirate sites serve malware), poisoning your users and your reputation. BUSINESS: SEO "Pirate Update" demotion + AdSense bans cut organic traffic, there's no defensible moat, and an illegal business has no legal exit/sale. NET: for a developer, the only paths without a near-fatal pitfall in some category are (a) a fully legal aggregator that links out / uses licensed APIs, or (b) self-hosting your own legally-acquired library — both of which sidestep the felony, the extractor treadmill, and the deplatforming cascade.

components.name: The extractor breakage treadmill (MegaCloud/RapidCloud decryptor rot)
components.role: TECHNICAL — the #1 maintenance killer. Pirate video hosts (MegaCloud, the backend USTR says feeds 260+ pirate sites with 46,000+ movies / 16,000+ TV series) deliberately rotate the AES key, obfuscate the JS that derives it, and change embed domains to break scrapers. Each rotation silently 404s/black-screens every downstream site at once. Mitigation is a never-ending re-RE cycle; there is NO permanent fix because the upstream is adversarial. This is the dependency that makes a zoro/hianime clone un-ownable.
examples: megacloud.blog domain expired -> streams moved to megacloud.tv; sites with hardcoded old embed URLs all broke simultaneously (aniwatch issue #17, RAELIE1/MegaCloudFix, RichRADS YouTube 'MegaCloud Error fix')
Eggwite/megacloud-key-extractor (AST/pattern-matching key extractor) repo now explicitly labeled 'NO LONGER FUNCTIONAL' after a key-rotation
aniwatch-api fell back to fetching keys from yogesh-hacker/MegacloudKeys, and degraded to user-facing 'The Mega Link May Not Work, go with Alternative' messages
encryption-scheme change in MegaCloud broke streaming links across the entire consumet/aniwatch ecosystem at once

components.name: Cloudflare anti-bot / Turnstile blocking your scrapers
components.role: TECHNICAL — your scraper boxes must look like real browsers. Turnstile runs invisible JS/fingerprint challenges; HTTP-only clients (python requests, axios) fail instantly with no token. 2024-2025 detection got 'significantly more aggressive.' Mitigation = headless real browsers (Camoufox, SeleniumBase UC, Playwright-stealth), open-source solvers (FlareSolverr, Byparr), or paid 'Web Unlocker' APIs — all add latency, cost, and themselves break on each Cloudflare update. No durable free fix.
examples: Turnstile blocks scrapers before a single page loads; requires full JS environment to generate token
Byparr (Camoufox-based) and FlareSolverr cited as the strongest open-source solvers; still cat-and-mouse
Bright Data Web Unlocker positioned as the only tier that reliably handles Cloudflare/DataDome — highest cost

components.name: m3u8/HLS player layer: CORS, codec, mixed-content, segment failures
components.role: TECHNICAL — even with a working stream URL, the browser blocks it. HLS makes many requests (manifest, media playlist, segments, keys, subtitles) and each can fail CORS independently; hls.js uses XHR so the browser blocks cross-origin at the network layer. Forces you to run an m3u8 proxy that rewrites every segment URL through your server — which then carries ALL the video bandwidth (see proxy runaway). Mitigation exists for playback but not for the cost it creates.
examples: m3u8proxy / MetaHat m3u8-streaming-proxy / Flask proxies exist specifically to rewrite segment URLs and inject CORS headers
react-player issue #1699: m3u8 CORS failures
mixed-content blocking when stream is HTTP and site is HTTPS

components.name: Proxy bandwidth runaway
components.role: FINANCIAL+TECHNICAL — the moment you proxy m3u8 segments to fix CORS/hotlink protection, every byte of video transits YOUR server/CDN instead of the upstream's. A single 1080p anime episode is ~0.7-1.5 GB; at scale this is the dominant cost line. On egress-billed CDNs (CloudFront ~$0.085/GB) this explodes; mitigation is free-egress storage (Cloudflare R2, Bunny) or NOT proxying (302-redirect to upstream and accept breakage). Real tension with no clean answer.
examples: CloudFront ~$0.085/GB vs Cloudflare R2 $0 egress vs Bunny Stream $0.005/min — 50TB/mo = $4,000+ difference
cost spread across CloudFront/Cloudflare/Bunny/Fastly exceeded 18x at 200TB/mo for identical workload

components.name: Widevine L1 / hardware DRM you cannot bypass
components.role: TECHNICAL — the reason you scrape other pirates instead of ripping Crunchyroll/Netflix directly. L1 decrypts inside a hardware TEE; Netflix/Prime cap L3 (software CDM) to 720p and only send HD/4K to L1+HDCP2.2 chains. L3 CDM extraction exists (tomer8007/tbodt widevine-l3-decryptor) but is patched, limited to SD/720p, and is itself a DMCA 1201 circumvention act. NO realistic mitigation for HD legit-source ripping — which is exactly why the ecosystem is parasitic on hosts like MegaCloud and inherits their fragility.
examples: widevinecdm.dll is heavily obfuscated; L3 guessers/decryptors are routinely patched
Netflix caps L3 to 720p; 4K requires L1 + HDCP 2.2
DMCA 1201 makes the bypass itself a separate offense

components.name: Criminal liability: PLSA felony + real operator convictions
components.role: LEGAL — the existential pitfall, no technical mitigation. U.S. Protecting Lawful Streaming Act (Dec 2020) makes willful, for-profit commercial streaming a felony: up to 3 years first offense, 5 for pre-release works, up to 10 in aggravated cases. Real outcomes: Jetflicks' Kristopher Dallmann got 7 years (2025, 'largest internet piracy case ever to go to trial,' $37.5M est.); B9GOOD operator got 3 years suspended + RMB1.8M fine (China 2023, first CODA-driven overseas conviction); Fmovies operators arrested in Vietnam (2024). Only mitigation is don't operate it for profit / don't host or link infringing streams.
examples: PLSA: felony, up to 10 years aggravated; DOJ targets providers not users
Jetflicks: Dallmann 7 yrs, Huber 18 mo, Polo ~5 yrs, Villarino 12 mo
B9GOOD: 15-yr operator, 300M+ visits, 95% from Japan, sentenced Dec 2023

components.name: Takedown & enforcement machine: DMCA, USTR, MPA/ACE/CODA, GitHub purges, domain seizures
components.role: LEGAL/OPERATIONAL — coordinated rightsholder + government pressure. USTR Notorious Markets listing (HiAnime listed 2 years running; MegaCloud named as the backend; Vietnam flagged Priority Foreign Country) precedes shutdowns. ACE (MPA-led) assisted Vietnam in the Fmovies takedown. GitHub removed 900+ anime repos (incl. aniwatch, aniwatch-api, MegacloudKeys) in March 2026 on a Remove Your Media/Crunchyroll/VIZ notice. FBI Operation 404 and INTERPOL I-SOP seize domains/infrastructure. Mitigation: offshore + frequent domain hopping only delays it.
examples: GitHub 900+ repos nuked March 2026; framed as DMCA 1201 (GitHub rejected circumvention theory but removed on standard copyright grounds)
HiAnime + 9anime went dark ~March 2026 after USTR listing + Vietnam crackdown
AnimeHeaven (11.6M Apr visits) had all episodes nuked after Crunchyroll DMCA

components.name: Infrastructure deplatforming cascade (CDN/host/registrar)
components.role: OPERATIONAL — every vendor can cut you. Cloudflare has a DMCA repeat-infringer termination policy and forwards complaints upstream; a Japanese court found Cloudflare liable ~$24M (Nov 2025) for CDNing manga piracy, which pushes CDNs to drop pirate customers faster. Hosts ban accounts for piracy; registrars suspend domains under court order/seizure. Mitigation: piracy-tolerant offshore hosts + Cloudflare-in-front + constant domain rotation — fragile and escalating.
examples: Cloudflare repeat-infringer termination policy (17 USC 512); acts on court orders
~$24M Japan judgment vs Cloudflare (Nov 2025) for manga-piracy CDN
Vidsrc.to (Fmovies-linked host) going down took hundreds of downstream movie sites with it — single-point-of-failure risk

components.name: Payment processor & ad-network bans
components.role: OPERATIONAL/FINANCIAL — legit money rails refuse the category. Stripe and PayPal prohibit piracy/adult/'high-risk'; accounts get frozen with no appeal. Google AdSense stops serving ads on any DMCA'd page AND permanently blocks future AdSense on it. Mitigation = shady popunder/adult/crypto-pay processors at worse terms, which feeds the malvertising problem below.
examples: AdSense: permanent ad block on DMCA-noticed pages
Stripe/PayPal freeze high-risk/adult/piracy accounts, often no appeal
chargebacks >1% of volume -> processor flags account high-risk -> termination

components.name: Malvertising & shady ad networks (security/reputation)
components.role: SECURITY/REPUTATION — the monetization trap. Locked out of clean ad demand, you run popunder/redirect networks (Hilltopads, PopAds, ExoClick, EroAdvertising) that don't police creatives. Result: malvertising = ~12% of pirate-site ads (~$121M/yr industry-wide, ~$68.3M from US visits); ~8 in 10 pirate sites served malware. You infect your own users, destroy trust/retention, and invite security blacklisting. NO mitigation that preserves revenue — clean ads won't run on a pirate site.
examples: DCA/White Bullet: advertising fuels a $1.34B/yr illegal piracy market; malvertising ~12% / ~$121M
~80% of investigated pirate sites served malware-ridden ads; popunders open hidden windows users don't see
ExoClick/AdKernel/EvoLeads named reselling stolen pirate traffic

components.name: Scraper IP bans, hacks, and your own infrastructure as a target
components.role: SECURITY/OPERATIONAL — your scraping boxes get datacenter-IP-banned fast (datacenter proxies $0.066-0.09/GB but fail strict WAFs; residential $3-8.40/GB survive but expensive). As a high-traffic site run by an anonymous operator with sketchy ad code, you're also a juicy target to get hacked / have your panel popped, with no legit incident-response options. Mitigation: rotate residential proxies (costly) + harden, but anonymity limits your recourse.
examples: Bright Data: datacenter $0.066-0.077/GB (blocked easily) vs residential $5.88-8.40/GB PAYG, ~$3/GB committed; mobile $14.4/GB
datacenter IPs blocked by aggressive anti-bot; residential = hardest to detect

components.name: SEO 'Pirate' demotion, no moat, no exit (business)
components.role: BUSINESS — long-run value destruction. Google's 'Pirate Update' demotes whole sites with many valid DMCA notices, cutting the organic traffic that makes piracy profitable; delisting is called the single highest-leverage anti-piracy move. There's no defensible moat (catalog = everyone else's), retention erodes under malware + breakage + domain hops, and you can't legally sell/raise on an illegal business — zero exit. No mitigation; the asset is structurally worthless to acquire.
examples: Google copyright-demotion signal downgrades entire sites; AdSense auto-disabled on noticed pages
aniwatch's own lineage (zoro.to -> aniwatch.to -> hianime) shows the forced-rebrand churn that resets brand/SEO each time
an illegal operation has no clean acquirer, no bank, no equity exit

howItWorks: TECHNICAL FAILURE LOOP: Upstream pirate host (MegaCloud/RapidCloud) ships obfuscated JS that derives an AES key + encrypts the m3u8 URL. Your extractor reverse-engineers the key, decrypts, and serves the stream. The host periodically rotates the key, re-obfuscates the JS, or moves the embed domain (e.g. .blog->.tv). At that instant EVERY downstream clone breaks at once. You re-RE, ship a patch, and repeat — indefinitely, because the upstream is adversarial and unpaid maintainers eventually abandon (repos get archived or marked NO LONGER FUNCTIONAL).

SCRAPER FAILURE LOOP: To get the page you must beat Cloudflare Turnstile/bot-management, which needs a full JS/browser fingerprint. HTTP clients fail; you move to headless real browsers / solvers / paid unlockers. Each Cloudflare update + datacenter-IP reputation hit re-breaks you, pushing you to costlier residential proxies.

DELIVERY/COST LOOP: Fixing CORS/hotlink protection forces an m3u8 proxy, which routes all video bytes through you. On egress-billed CDNs this can exceed ad revenue; you migrate to free-egress storage (R2/Bunny) or stop proxying (and accept breakage). Either way bandwidth, not features, dominates economics.

LEGAL/OPERATIONAL CASCADE: Traffic grows -> USTR Notorious Markets listing -> rightsholder coalition (MPA/ACE, CODA, Crunchyroll via Remove Your Media) files DMCA + 1201 notices -> GitHub purges your tooling, AdSense disables pages, Google demotes you in search, CDN/host/registrar drop you, payment processors freeze funds -> domain seizure / arrest. Each rebrand+domain hop (zoro->aniwatch->hianime) buys time but resets SEO/brand and never stops the cascade.

MONETIZATION TRAP: Banned from clean ads and processors, you adopt popunder/malvertising networks and crypto/high-risk payments. This injects malware to users, raises chargebacks (>1% = processor death), tanks retention/reputation, and reinforces the 'notorious market' designation — a self-feeding doom loop.

keyRepos.name: ghoshRitesh12/aniwatch (+ aniwatch-api)

Both were named in the March 2026 Remove Your Media DMCA notice and were among the 900+ repos GitHub removed. Issue #17 documents the MegaCloud encryption/domain breakage. Concrete proof of both the technical treadmill and the legal takedown risk.

keyRepos.name: consumet/consumet.ts
keyRepos.whatItProvides: Multi-provider scraping library (zoro/anime among providers) whose releases are largely a history of fixing broken extractors after upstream rotations — illustrates the maintenance treadmill firsthand.

keyRepos.name: RAELIE1/MegaCloudFix & yogesh-hacker/MegacloudKeys

keyRepos.name: tomer8007/widevine-l3-decryptor (and tbodt fork)
