technologies: MegaCloud/RapidCloud (upstream pirate video hosts; AES-encrypted m3u8 + obfuscated JS key derivation)
HLS / m3u8 / hls.js (multi-request streaming; CORS + mixed-content failure surface)
Cloudflare Turnstile + Bot Management (anti-scraper)
Headless browsers & solvers: Camoufox, SeleniumBase UC, Playwright-stealth, FlareSolverr, Byparr
Proxy networks: Bright Data (datacenter $0.066-0.09/GB, residential $3-8.40/GB, mobile $14.4/GB, Web Unlocker)
CDN/storage: Cloudflare R2 ($0.015/GB-mo, $0 egress), Bunny Stream ($0.005/min), AWS CloudFront (~$0.085/GB), Cloudflare Stream ($0.01/min delivered)
Widevine DRM (L1 hardware TEE vs L3 software CDM); HDCP 2.2
Ad networks: Hilltopads, PopAds, ExoClick, EroAdvertising (popunder/malvertising); Google AdSense (auto-disabled on DMCA pages)
Payment rails: Stripe/PayPal (category-banned), high-risk + crypto processors
Enforcement infra: USTR Notorious Markets / Special 301, MPA-led ACE, CODA, FBI Operation 404, INTERPOL I-SOP, GitHub DMCA/1201 process
gotchas: The extractor will break with ZERO warning and ALL clones break simultaneously — it is not a bug you can prevent, the upstream rotates keys/domains specifically to break you. There is no permanent fix, only a perpetual re-RE treadmill; unpaid maintainers eventually quit (repos archived / 'NO LONGER FUNCTIONAL').

Operating for profit flips this from civil DMCA to a U.S. FELONY under the PLSA (up to 10 yrs). Real people are in prison (Dallmann, 7 yrs; B9GOOD operator, 3 yrs). 'It's just an aggregator/I only link' is not reliable cover once you host streams or proxy bytes.

Proxying m3u8 to fix CORS quietly makes YOU pay for all video bandwidth — pick free-egress (R2/Bunny) or you'll get a five-figure CloudFront bill; egress is ~80% of media cost and providers differ up to 18x.

You will be forced into malvertising: clean ad networks and Stripe/PayPal refuse the category, so popunder/malware ad networks are the only revenue — meaning you actively harm your own users (~80% of pirate sites serve malware) and torch retention.

Cloudflare/CDN/host/registrar can all drop you, AND Cloudflare itself is now being held liable (~$24M Japan, Nov 2025), so CDNs are getting faster at terminating pirate customers — your 'Cloudflare in front' shield is weakening.
GitHub will delete your tooling on a single coalition notice (900+ repos in one March 2026 sweep) — keep mirrors, but assume the public ecosystem is impermanent.
Google's Pirate Update + AdSense page-bans destroy your two main growth/revenue channels; combined with forced domain hops (zoro->aniwatch->hianime), you can never compound brand or SEO equity.
There is NO exit: you cannot legally sell, raise money on, or bank an infringing business; the asset is worthless to any legitimate acquirer.
Widevine L1 is effectively unbreakable for an individual — you cannot rip HD from legit sources, so you're permanently dependent on (and as fragile as) other pirates' hosts.

SAFE-PATH takeaway: a fully legal aggregator (link-out / licensed APIs / metadata only) or self-hosting your own legally-acquired library avoids the felony, the extractor treadmill, the malvertising trap, AND the deplatforming cascade at once — it is the only design that isn't fatally exposed in some category.

refuted.claim: Claim 3: The ~$24M (Nov 2025) Japanese court judgment against Cloudflare for manga-piracy CDN is reported secondhand; appeal status and final amount UNVERIFIED.
refuted.note: DOLLAR FIGURE IS WRONG — this is the most important correction. The Tokyo District Court judgment (19 Nov 2025) ordered Cloudflare to pay ~500 million yen, about US$3.2-3.3M, NOT ~$24M. The ~$24M figure is roughly 7-8x too high. (The court recognized ~3.6 billion yen in total damages for four works, but plaintiffs Shueisha/Kodansha/Shogakukan/Kadokawa only claimed a portion, so the order is ~500M yen.) The hedges in the claim ARE correct: it is secondhand-reported, it is the first time a global CDN was held liable, and it is NOT final — Cloudflare plans to appeal (TorrentFreak). Date (Nov 2025), defendant, and 'not final / appeal pending / secondhand' are all accurate; only the dollar amount is materially off.

confirmed: Claim 1: HiAnime and 9anime shut down ~March 2026 — widely reported (CBR, Outlook Respawn) but treat 'permanently dead' as UNCONFIRMED given hints of possible return and successor/mirror domains.
Claim 2: '900+ repos removed' is TorrentFreak's figure from the March 2026 GitHub disclosure; exact count and which forks may have been reinstated are fluid.
Claim 4: Fmovies operators were 'arrested' in Vietnam (Aug 2024) but reports note they had 'not been charged' — conviction status UNCONFIRMED.
Claim 5: Specific current proxy/CDN/ad CPM prices (Bright Data, Bunny, R2, CloudFront) are 2025-2026 list prices and change frequently — re-verify before relying.
Claim 6: Malvertising '12% / $121M' and 'piracy = $1.34B/yr' come from 2021-2022 DCA/White Bullet reports — directionally valid but dated.

Claim 7: Whether a 'link-only / licensed-API aggregator' is actually legal is jurisdiction-dependent and fact-specific (linking to infringing content has been found infringing in some EU/US cases) — NOT legal advice; consult a lawyer.
corrections: CRITICAL (dollar figure): The Cloudflare manga-piracy judgment is ~500 million yen / ~US$3.2-3.3 million, NOT ~$24 million. The overview's '~$24M Japanese judgment' is off by roughly 7-8x. The court acknowledged ~3.6 billion yen in total damages for four works, but the plaintiffs (Shueisha, Kodansha, Shogakukan, Kadokawa) only claimed a portion, so the order was ~500M yen. Date (19 Nov 2025), 'first global CDN held liable,' and 'Cloudflare plans to appeal (not final)' are all correct — only the headline dollar amount must be fixed.

Wording: '9anime ... shut down ~March 2026' is imprecise. 9anime (rebranded AniWave) was shut down in the 2024 ACE anti-piracy wave. The March 2026 shutdown is HiAnime (the Zoro.to -> Aniwatch -> HiAnime lineage). Keep the two timelines distinct.

Bunny pricing units: the overview's 'Bunny ~$0.005/min' refers to Bunny Stream (per delivered video-minute), while Bunny CDN is ~$0.005/GB in EU/NA. Both exist; just don't conflate per-minute (Stream) with per-GB (CDN) when comparing against CloudFront's $0.085/GB.

Minor framing: '900+ repos removed' is more precisely '900+ forks across the targeted repositories' per TorrentFreak/GitHub, and GitHub explicitly rejected the DMCA 1201 anti-circumvention claim while still removing the repos on other copyright grounds — worth stating because it undercuts the 'MegacloudKeys = circumvention tool' legal theory.
additionalFindings: HiAnime was on the USTR Notorious Markets list in BOTH 2024 and 2025 (and a 2025 'Priority Notorious Linking and Streaming Sites' category), supporting the overview's 'two years on the USTR list' claim. It also appeared on the European Commission's piracy watchlist. Traffic at shutdown was large: ~244M visits in Aug 2025 / ~150M+ monthly cited at takedown.

Protecting Lawful Streaming Act penalty tiers verified exactly: up to 3 years (base), up to 5 years (works being prepared for commercial public performance / pre-release), up to 10 years (second/subsequent offense). Signed 27 Dec 2020 as part of the Consolidated Appropriations Act 2021; converted illegal commercial streaming from misdemeanor to felony.

Jetflicks: Kristopher Dallmann sentenced to 84 months (7 years) — confirmed (TorrentFreak/DOJ, July 2025; surrender date 17 Oct 2025). He was convicted June 2024; five defendants total sentenced; govt estimated $37.5M in infringement value. The '7 years (2025)' claim is solid.

B9GOOD / CODA: verified but with nuance. The operator ('Male A') was sentenced 26 Dec 2023 to 3 years imprisonment SUSPENDED for 3 years 6 months, plus an RMB 1.8M fine — i.e., a suspended sentence, not 3 years of actual incarceration. The overview's 'got 3 years (China, 2023, CODA case)' is directionally right but the suspension is a material detail. Two female uploaders also received suspended terms.

Eggwite megacloud-key-extractor 'NO LONGER FUNCTIONAL' confirmed verbatim in the GitHub repo title. The megacloud.blog -> megacloud.tv domain migration is real (megacloud.blog expired); community fixes (MegaCloudFix, Tampermonkey redirect scripts) exist to rewrite .blog to .tv — concrete evidence of the 'breakage treadmill' thesis.

Widevine L1 'effectively unbreakable for a hobbyist' is well-supported: L1 relies on hardware TEE with keys embedded in the processor, designed to block both screen-recording and high-quality downloads — consistent with the overview's point that pirates scrape other pirates rather than break L1 themselves.

Phishing-clone risk corroborates the SECURITY domain: post-shutdown HiAnime clones (.dk/.se/.cv) are flagged as suspected phishing/malware — an additional reputational/user-safety pitfall beyond the malvertising figures.
